0: <title>Life cycle assessment of sheep cheese production in a small dairy factory from Romanian rural area </title>
Received: 1 October 2021 / Accepted: 17 August 2022 / Published online: 26 August 2022 © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2022
1: The paper aims to analyse the life cycle of sheep milk cheese production, to investigate supply chain of cheese and to sug- gest improvements for minimizing the environmental impacts associated with this agri-food product. The functional unit chosen was 1 kg of sheep milk cheese. Modelling of the production process was performed based on real data obtained from a cheese production company in Romania. Two methods (CML and ReCiPe), included in GaBi software, were used for the environmental impact assessment. The following impacts: abiotic depletion potential elements, acidification potential, eutrophication potential, global warming potential, human toxicity potential (included in CML method) and climate change, fossil depletion, freshwater eutrophication, particulate matter formation, photochemical oxidant formation, terrestrial acidi- fication (included in ReCiPe method), were calculated. Results highlighted that energy production and diesel consumption are the main activities which contribute to the environmental impacts associated with cheese product. Emissions from diesel production and consumption contribute to global warming potential with 11.70% and 82.36%, respectively. Wastewater treatment mainly contributes to freshwater eutrophication with 43.58%, followed by raw milk production (37.52%), diesel production (12.34%) and others. The results of this study showed that the values for most impact categories decrease when the energy is obtained by renewable sources compared to the energy produced by conventional procedures. It was concluded that, in order to reduce the environmental impacts associated with sheep cheese production, the energy used in the process needs to be more environmentally friendly.
Keywords Dairy industry . Environmental impacts . Life cycle . Milk . Sheep cheese

3: <header>Introduction </header>

Worldwide, there is a constant increase in demand for dairy products (Canellada et al. 2018; Ghinea and Leahu 2020; Pulina et al. 2018; Rivas et al. 2019). Sheep milk has major nutritional value compared to cow and goat milks, due to high levels of protein, fat and calcium, which makes this type of milk excellent for cheese production (Balthazar et al. 2017). Mohapatra et al. (2019) indicated that sheep milk contains between 4.50 and 6.60% proteins, 6.99% fat, 3.90-4.90% lactose and 82% water, while Balthazar et al.

(2017) reported a content of 5.5% proteins, 5.9% fat, 4.8% lactose and 82.9% water. Most abundant protein from sheep milk is casein (4.2 to 5.2 g/100 g, representing around 80% of the total milk protein), which is very important in cheese production (Balthazar et al. 2017; Moatsou and Sakkas 2019; Park et al. 2007). The chemical composition of sheep milk depends on several factors like individuals, animal age, nutrition, lactation, season, environmental temperature, management, treatment and others (Balthazar et al. 2017; Mohapatra et al. 2019).

In 2018, the world production of sheep milk was esti- mated by FAOSTAT (2020) at 10,225,238 tonnes. In EU28, in the same year the fresh sheep milk production was regis- tered at 2,844,990 tonnes, while in Romania, 626,145 tonnes of sheep milk was obtained. Yearly, an average of 89 L of milk per ewe is obtained in Romania, much lower, compara- tive with 106 L of milk per ewe in Greece or 243 L milk per ewe in Spain (Pulina et al. 2018). At European level, in 2019, Greece was the main producer of sheep milk, followed by
Responsible Editor: Philippe Loubet
Cristina Ghinea cristina.ghinea@fia.usv.ro; cbghinea@yahoo.com 1 Faculty of Food Engineering, Stefan Cel Mare University of Suceava, 13 Universitatii Street, 720229 Suceava, Romania

Spain, Italy, Romania, France, Bulgaria and Portugal. From Fig. 1a, it can be observed that the total quantity of sheep milk produced in Romania decreased from 673,477 tonnes in 2014 to 425,500 tonnes in 2019. At global level, 726,421 tonnes of sheep cheese were produced in 2018, from which 402,743 tonnes of cheese from sheep milk were obtained only in the EU28 (FAOSTAT 2020). Approximately, 24,025 tonnes of sheep cheese were obtained in Romania, in 2018. According to FAOSTAT (2020), sheep cheese production in Romania has increased in recent years (Fig. 1b). In Roma- nia, cheese consumption is estimated at 5 kg /year per per- son. According to FRD Center (2016), 86% from Romanian consumers prefers cheese from cow milk, 53% cheese from sheep milk and 20% cheese from goat milk.

As all human activities, milk processing and dairy indus- try generate environmental impacts. Producers, consumers and other stakeholders believe that it is necessary to reduce these environmental impacts and increase sustainability (Cabral et al. 2020). The life cycle of products can be evalu- ated from environmental point of view by using life cycle

assessment (LCA); for example, the environmental impacts of a Romanian pork meat product (with pig farm, slaugh- terhouse and meat processing, transport and wastewater treatment, incineration of organic waste included in the system boundaries) were investigated by Ghinea and Leahu (2018), while the environmental footprints of a Romanian yogurt type (with activities such as milk processing, trans- port, solid waste and wastewater treatments included in the system boundaries) were determined by Ghinea and Leahu (2020). Different authors had used LCA for environmental impact assessment associated with sheep milk and cheese production (Mondello et al. 2018; Nunes et al. 2020; Vag- noni et al. 2017). The contribution of sheep milk production to the climate change was investigated by Sabia et al. (2020) considering four dairy sheep Italian family farms located in Basilicata Region, while Vagnoni and Franca (2018) investigated the contribution of two different production systems adopted to the same dairy sheep farm located in north-western Sardinia. Also, Batalla et al. (2015) calculated the carbon footprint of 12 individual Spanish dairy sheep

"farms, while 15 milk sheep farms have been surveyed by Escribano et al. (2020) in the same purpose. The carbon footprint values determined, expressed in kg of CO2 equiva- lents (CO2 eq), were 3.78 kg CO2 eq/ 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) according to Sabia et al. (2020), 3.25 kg CO2 eq/1 kg of FPCM (Vagnoni and Franca 2018), between 2.61 and 5.17 kg CO2 eq/1 kg of FPCM (Batalla et al. 2015) and from 1.77 to 4.09 kg CO2 eq/ kg of milk (Escribano et al. 2020). Sabia et al. (2020) stated that enteric emissions are the major contributor to the carbon footprint, while Batalla et al. (2015) showed that more intensive farms have lower carbon footprint values than more traditional farms, but when soil carbon sequestration is included there are no statistically significant difference between groups. The hotspots identified by Vagnoni and Franca (2018) are methane enteric emissions and imported soybean meal use; however, their study did not account carbon sequestration. Escribano et al. (2020) concluded that enteric fermentation, feed manufacture and transport are mainly responsible for the carbon footprint of sheep's milk and that in extensive farms the carbon sequestration by the soil is significant and contributes significantly to reduction of greenhouse gases emission. Mondello et al. (2018) investigated the environ- mental burden of traditional Italian sheep cheese (Tuscan ""Pecorino"") production and proposed some improvement like: the use of an extensive breeding system and a pho- tovoltaic system for environmental impacts reduction. The environmental impacts associated with production of a traditional Portuguese sheep cheese (""Beira Baixa"") were determined by Nunes et al. (2020), while Vagnoni et al. (2017) compared the environmental profile of two Italian sheep cheese: ""Pecorino di Osilo"" a semi-artisanal typical cheese and ""Pecorino Romano"" cheese produced at indus- trial level. Functional units chosen by these authors were: 1 kg of sheep cheese (Mondello et al. 2018; Vagnoni et al. 2017), 1 kg of sheep milk and 1 kg of sheep cheese (Nunes et al. 2020). The steps considered in the system boundaries by Mondello et al. (2018) were breeding, milk transport and cheese production, while Vagnoni et al. (2017) considered for the evaluation beside production, collection of milk and cheese production, also the cheese distribution step. Nunes et al. (2020) included in the system boundaries milk produc- tion stage divided in three sub-stages (concentrated feed pro- duction, fodder production and milk production) and sheep cheese production. Mondello et al. (2018) and Vagnoni et al. (2017) used SimaPro software for determination of environ- mental impacts associated with production of sheep cheese, while Nunes et al. (2020) chosen GaBi software. Even though the authors used the same software, they chose dif- ferent methods for evaluation; for example, Mondello et al. (2018) used ReCiPe midpoint H method and considered all 18 impact categories included in this method in order to achieve a higher level of detail, while Vagnoni et al. (2017)"|"chose IPCC especially for the carbon footprint estimates and CML-IA version 3.3 for others categories (abiotic deple- tion, human toxicity, photochemical oxidation, acidification, eutrophication and impact categories regarding the environ- mental ecotoxicity). The hotspots identified by Mondello et al. (2018) and Vagnoni et al. (2017) were especially feed production and electricity consumption. Wastewater treat- ment was indicated by Mondello et al. (2018), also, as one of the hotspots. They proposed some performance improve- ment regarding the feed used, or forage species and also to change the conventional electricity source with green electricity sources. Vagnoni et al. (2017) even developed and evaluated four scenarios with different percentages of electricity from photovoltaic or wind power sources and con- cluded that the results are not statistically different. Nunes et al. (2020) compared their results with those obtained in other studies and concluded that milk production phase is the large contributor to climate change, terrestrial acidifica- tion and marine eutrophication. In Romania, there is a great tradition and experience for sheep production (Ilisiu et al. 2012). In 2020, there were approximately 10.5 million sheep in the country (Statista 2020). According to Juler (2014) about half of all sheep in Romania are kept for milk (turned into cheese) rather than for meat. The milk production and composition varies, depending on the breed of sheep: between 100 and 140 L (7.7% fat, 6.0% proteins, 16.65% total solids) for ""Turcana"" (the most important breed in Romania with 65% of sheep stock), and 70-90 L (7.0% fat, 6.5% proteins, 15.02% total solids) for ""Tigaie"" (Lujerdean et al. 2009). Most of the milk production is processed artisanal in small factories (Ilisiu et al. 2012), and according to Muregan et al. (2021), the cheese production in Romania has increased in recent years due to the development of small local factories. In Suceava county, the second largest county in Romania (8553.5 square kilometres), with a mountain area of 53%, plateau area 30% and meadow area 17%, the pastoral potential is high, and animal breeding is the preferential agricultural activity and an ancient occupation of the inhabitants (Suceava 2022). According to Grodea (2016), the highest concentration of the processing dairy factories approved for intra-community trade was found in the Suceava county. One typical Roma- nian cheese, with a specific recipe and technological manu- facturing process presented in the materials and methods section, was selected for this study. The chosen cheese is a traditional product, namely ""cas"", and from the ""cas"" whey is obtained ""urda"", a product relatively rich in pro- tein and low in fat. The characteristics of mountain ""cas"" according to ROMONTANA (2016), for 100 g, are: average nutritional value 1300 kj-300 kcal, dry matter 50%, 20 g protein, 28 g lipids, 0 g carbohydrates and 4.5 pH. Validity is 30 days, with storage between and + 6 °C. ""Urda"" is made up of milk lactalbumin, which remains in the whey"

after coagulation of casein. The sweet whey is heated to 85-90 acid whey (12%) is added, and after precipitation of lactalbumin and removal of the whey, it is placed in thick cloth bags to drain. The "urda" is white, with a soft, buttery consistency, sweet taste and the smell of boiled milk, and has the shape of the bags in which it was drained. It has a soft, crumbly texture. From 10-12 L of whey usually results 1 kg of "urda". The characteristics of the mountain "urda" for 100 g are as follows: 190 kcal average nutritional value, 40% dry matter, 18 g protein, 8 g lipids, 5 g carbohydrates and pH 5.5 (ROMONTANA 2016). Validity is 4 days as such and 8 days in vacuum packed, with storage between + 4 and °C. An important aspect of environmental protection is that the whey is not discharged in the form of concentrated wastewater, but is used to obtain another type of cheese. "Urda" cheese production is practiced in mountainous areas in Romania (Iligiu et al. 2012), Montenegro (Bojanic Raso- vic et al. 2017), Macedonia (Teneva-Angelova et al. 2018) and Greece (Pappa et al. 2020). Sheep's milk was obtained from sheep that spend the summer grazing in the Carpathian Mountains, and in winter they go down to the shelters in the valley. The type of energy consumed is one of the most important sources of environmental impacts associated with production of cheese. In 2015, the main energy resource in Romania was natural gas (29% in the primary energy mix) followed by crude oil (27%). However, Romania is rich in renewable energy resources as: wind, photovoltaic energy, biomass, hydropower, geothermal potential (Dragomir et al. 2016; Cîrstea et al. 2018; Vrínceanu et al. 2022). It has the highest potential in Southeast Europe in the field of wind energy, with high energy potential in the littoral and coastal areas, but also in alpine areas with plateaus and mountain valleys. Benefiting from 210 sunny days a year, Romania has a wide solar potential spread all over the country. Wind energy is produced in Romania starting from 2007 (annual wind potential approximately 23 TWh), while until 2017 photovoltaic power stations or photovoltaic (PV) parks with a combined installed capacity of 4871.66 MW were built. In north-east of the country, there are 26 PV farms with 28.15 MW installed capacity (Cîrstea et al. 2018). Accord- ing to Cîrstea et al. (2018) production of biogas and bio- fuels in Romania has high potential and the issue of waste management can be solved by energy and biogas produc- tion. Biogas for heat and power can be produced through anaerobic digestion of biomass waste. Achinas et al. (2018) demonstrated that biogas can be obtained from sheep manure co-digestion with cow dung, while Cestonaro et al. (2015) produced biogas and biofertilizer through anaerobic co-digestion of sheep bedding with cow manure. Alkhamis et al. (2021) indicated that sheep manure production can be 0.42 kg manure/head/day and estimated that 258 L biogas/ kg manure can be obtained at standard temperature and pres- sure conditions.

In this context, the present study provides valuable infor- mation for dairy industry, especially for those who want to reduce the environmental impacts associated with cheese production. This study is based on real data used for deter- mination of hotspots, which can conduct to economic and environmental benefits, enlarge database information and increase knowledge about the environmental performance of mountain dairy systems. The study includes a sensitivity analysis for transportation of sheep cheese produced in the mountain area and three scenarios which propose the uti- lization of energy from renewable sources in exchange for energy consumption from conventional sources.

(2017) reported a content of 5.5% proteins, 5.9% fat, 4.8% lactose and 82.9% water. Most abundant protein from sheep milk is casein (4.2 to 5.2 g/100 g, representing around 80% of the total milk protein), which is very important in cheese production (Balthazar et al. 2017; Moatsou and Sakkas 2019; Park et al. 2007). The chemical composition of sheep milk depends on several factors like individuals, animal age, nutrition, lactation, season, environmental temperature, management, treatment and others (Balthazar et al. 2017; Mohapatra et al. 2019).

In 2018, the world production of sheep milk was esti- mated by FAOSTAT (2020) at 10,225,238 tonnes. In EU28, in the same year the fresh sheep milk production was regis- tered at 2,844,990 tonnes, while in Romania, 626,145 tonnes of sheep milk was obtained. Yearly, an average of 89 L of milk per ewe is obtained in Romania, much lower, compara- tive with 106 L of milk per ewe in Greece or 243 L milk per ewe in Spain (Pulina et al. 2018). At European level, in 2019, Greece was the main producer of sheep milk, followed by
Responsible Editor: Philippe Loubet
Cristina Ghinea cristina.ghinea@fia.usv.ro; cbghinea@yahoo.com 1 Faculty of Food Engineering, Stefan Cel Mare University of Suceava, 13 Universitatii Street, 720229 Suceava, Romania

Spain, Italy, Romania, France, Bulgaria and Portugal. From Fig. 1a, it can be observed that the total quantity of sheep milk produced in Romania decreased from 673,477 tonnes in 2014 to 425,500 tonnes in 2019. At global level, 726,421 tonnes of sheep cheese were produced in 2018, from which 402,743 tonnes of cheese from sheep milk were obtained only in the EU28 (FAOSTAT 2020). Approximately, 24,025 tonnes of sheep cheese were obtained in Romania, in 2018. According to FAOSTAT (2020), sheep cheese production in Romania has increased in recent years (Fig. 1b). In Roma- nia, cheese consumption is estimated at 5 kg /year per per- son. According to FRD Center (2016), 86% from Romanian consumers prefers cheese from cow milk, 53% cheese from sheep milk and 20% cheese from goat milk.

As all human activities, milk processing and dairy indus- try generate environmental impacts. Producers, consumers and other stakeholders believe that it is necessary to reduce these environmental impacts and increase sustainability (Cabral et al. 2020). The life cycle of products can be evalu- ated from environmental point of view by using life cycle

assessment (LCA); for example, the environmental impacts of a Romanian pork meat product (with pig farm, slaugh- terhouse and meat processing, transport and wastewater treatment, incineration of organic waste included in the system boundaries) were investigated by Ghinea and Leahu (2018), while the environmental footprints of a Romanian yogurt type (with activities such as milk processing, trans- port, solid waste and wastewater treatments included in the system boundaries) were determined by Ghinea and Leahu (2020). Different authors had used LCA for environmental impact assessment associated with sheep milk and cheese production (Mondello et al. 2018; Nunes et al. 2020; Vag- noni et al. 2017). The contribution of sheep milk production to the climate change was investigated by Sabia et al. (2020) considering four dairy sheep Italian family farms located in Basilicata Region, while Vagnoni and Franca (2018) investigated the contribution of two different production systems adopted to the same dairy sheep farm located in north-western Sardinia. Also, Batalla et al. (2015) calculated the carbon footprint of 12 individual Spanish dairy sheep

"farms, while 15 milk sheep farms have been surveyed by Escribano et al. (2020) in the same purpose. The carbon footprint values determined, expressed in kg of CO2 equiva- lents (CO2 eq), were 3.78 kg CO2 eq/ 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) according to Sabia et al. (2020), 3.25 kg CO2 eq/1 kg of FPCM (Vagnoni and Franca 2018), between 2.61 and 5.17 kg CO2 eq/1 kg of FPCM (Batalla et al. 2015) and from 1.77 to 4.09 kg CO2 eq/ kg of milk (Escribano et al. 2020). Sabia et al. (2020) stated that enteric emissions are the major contributor to the carbon footprint, while Batalla et al. (2015) showed that more intensive farms have lower carbon footprint values than more traditional farms, but when soil carbon sequestration is included there are no statistically significant difference between groups. The hotspots identified by Vagnoni and Franca (2018) are methane enteric emissions and imported soybean meal use; however, their study did not account carbon sequestration. Escribano et al. (2020) concluded that enteric fermentation, feed manufacture and transport are mainly responsible for the carbon footprint of sheep's milk and that in extensive farms the carbon sequestration by the soil is significant and contributes significantly to reduction of greenhouse gases emission. Mondello et al. (2018) investigated the environ- mental burden of traditional Italian sheep cheese (Tuscan ""Pecorino"") production and proposed some improvement like: the use of an extensive breeding system and a pho- tovoltaic system for environmental impacts reduction. The environmental impacts associated with production of a traditional Portuguese sheep cheese (""Beira Baixa"") were determined by Nunes et al. (2020), while Vagnoni et al. (2017) compared the environmental profile of two Italian sheep cheese: ""Pecorino di Osilo"" a semi-artisanal typical cheese and ""Pecorino Romano"" cheese produced at indus- trial level. Functional units chosen by these authors were: 1 kg of sheep cheese (Mondello et al. 2018; Vagnoni et al. 2017), 1 kg of sheep milk and 1 kg of sheep cheese (Nunes et al. 2020). The steps considered in the system boundaries by Mondello et al. (2018) were breeding, milk transport and cheese production, while Vagnoni et al. (2017) considered for the evaluation beside production, collection of milk and cheese production, also the cheese distribution step. Nunes et al. (2020) included in the system boundaries milk produc- tion stage divided in three sub-stages (concentrated feed pro- duction, fodder production and milk production) and sheep cheese production. Mondello et al. (2018) and Vagnoni et al. (2017) used SimaPro software for determination of environ- mental impacts associated with production of sheep cheese, while Nunes et al. (2020) chosen GaBi software. Even though the authors used the same software, they chose dif- ferent methods for evaluation; for example, Mondello et al. (2018) used ReCiPe midpoint H method and considered all 18 impact categories included in this method in order to achieve a higher level of detail, while Vagnoni et al. (2017)"|"chose IPCC especially for the carbon footprint estimates and CML-IA version 3.3 for others categories (abiotic deple- tion, human toxicity, photochemical oxidation, acidification, eutrophication and impact categories regarding the environ- mental ecotoxicity). The hotspots identified by Mondello et al. (2018) and Vagnoni et al. (2017) were especially feed production and electricity consumption. Wastewater treat- ment was indicated by Mondello et al. (2018), also, as one of the hotspots. They proposed some performance improve- ment regarding the feed used, or forage species and also to change the conventional electricity source with green electricity sources. Vagnoni et al. (2017) even developed and evaluated four scenarios with different percentages of electricity from photovoltaic or wind power sources and con- cluded that the results are not statistically different. Nunes et al. (2020) compared their results with those obtained in other studies and concluded that milk production phase is the large contributor to climate change, terrestrial acidifica- tion and marine eutrophication. In Romania, there is a great tradition and experience for sheep production (Ilisiu et al. 2012). In 2020, there were approximately 10.5 million sheep in the country (Statista 2020). According to Juler (2014) about half of all sheep in Romania are kept for milk (turned into cheese) rather than for meat. The milk production and composition varies, depending on the breed of sheep: between 100 and 140 L (7.7% fat, 6.0% proteins, 16.65% total solids) for ""Turcana"" (the most important breed in Romania with 65% of sheep stock), and 70-90 L (7.0% fat, 6.5% proteins, 15.02% total solids) for ""Tigaie"" (Lujerdean et al. 2009). Most of the milk production is processed artisanal in small factories (Ilisiu et al. 2012), and according to Muregan et al. (2021), the cheese production in Romania has increased in recent years due to the development of small local factories. In Suceava county, the second largest county in Romania (8553.5 square kilometres), with a mountain area of 53%, plateau area 30% and meadow area 17%, the pastoral potential is high, and animal breeding is the preferential agricultural activity and an ancient occupation of the inhabitants (Suceava 2022). According to Grodea (2016), the highest concentration of the processing dairy factories approved for intra-community trade was found in the Suceava county. One typical Roma- nian cheese, with a specific recipe and technological manu- facturing process presented in the materials and methods section, was selected for this study. The chosen cheese is a traditional product, namely ""cas"", and from the ""cas"" whey is obtained ""urda"", a product relatively rich in pro- tein and low in fat. The characteristics of mountain ""cas"" according to ROMONTANA (2016), for 100 g, are: average nutritional value 1300 kj-300 kcal, dry matter 50%, 20 g protein, 28 g lipids, 0 g carbohydrates and 4.5 pH. Validity is 30 days, with storage between and + 6 °C. ""Urda"" is made up of milk lactalbumin, which remains in the whey"

after coagulation of casein. The sweet whey is heated to 85-90 acid whey (12%) is added, and after precipitation of lactalbumin and removal of the whey, it is placed in thick cloth bags to drain. The "urda" is white, with a soft, buttery consistency, sweet taste and the smell of boiled milk, and has the shape of the bags in which it was drained. It has a soft, crumbly texture. From 10-12 L of whey usually results 1 kg of "urda". The characteristics of the mountain "urda" for 100 g are as follows: 190 kcal average nutritional value, 40% dry matter, 18 g protein, 8 g lipids, 5 g carbohydrates and pH 5.5 (ROMONTANA 2016). Validity is 4 days as such and 8 days in vacuum packed, with storage between + 4 and °C. An important aspect of environmental protection is that the whey is not discharged in the form of concentrated wastewater, but is used to obtain another type of cheese. "Urda" cheese production is practiced in mountainous areas in Romania (Iligiu et al. 2012), Montenegro (Bojanic Raso- vic et al. 2017), Macedonia (Teneva-Angelova et al. 2018) and Greece (Pappa et al. 2020). Sheep's milk was obtained from sheep that spend the summer grazing in the Carpathian Mountains, and in winter they go down to the shelters in the valley. The type of energy consumed is one of the most important sources of environmental impacts associated with production of cheese. In 2015, the main energy resource in Romania was natural gas (29% in the primary energy mix) followed by crude oil (27%). However, Romania is rich in renewable energy resources as: wind, photovoltaic energy, biomass, hydropower, geothermal potential (Dragomir et al. 2016; Cîrstea et al. 2018; Vrínceanu et al. 2022). It has the highest potential in Southeast Europe in the field of wind energy, with high energy potential in the littoral and coastal areas, but also in alpine areas with plateaus and mountain valleys. Benefiting from 210 sunny days a year, Romania has a wide solar potential spread all over the country. Wind energy is produced in Romania starting from 2007 (annual wind potential approximately 23 TWh), while until 2017 photovoltaic power stations or photovoltaic (PV) parks with a combined installed capacity of 4871.66 MW were built. In north-east of the country, there are 26 PV farms with 28.15 MW installed capacity (Cîrstea et al. 2018). Accord- ing to Cîrstea et al. (2018) production of biogas and bio- fuels in Romania has high potential and the issue of waste management can be solved by energy and biogas produc- tion. Biogas for heat and power can be produced through anaerobic digestion of biomass waste. Achinas et al. (2018) demonstrated that biogas can be obtained from sheep manure co-digestion with cow dung, while Cestonaro et al. (2015) produced biogas and biofertilizer through anaerobic co-digestion of sheep bedding with cow manure. Alkhamis et al. (2021) indicated that sheep manure production can be 0.42 kg manure/head/day and estimated that 258 L biogas/ kg manure can be obtained at standard temperature and pres- sure conditions.

In this context, the present study provides valuable infor- mation for dairy industry, especially for those who want to reduce the environmental impacts associated with cheese production. This study is based on real data used for deter- mination of hotspots, which can conduct to economic and environmental benefits, enlarge database information and increase knowledge about the environmental performance of mountain dairy systems. The study includes a sensitivity analysis for transportation of sheep cheese produced in the mountain area and three scenarios which propose the uti- lization of energy from renewable sources in exchange for energy consumption from conventional sources.

The carbon footprint values determined, expressed in kg of CO2 equiva- lents (CO2 eq), were 3.78 kg CO2 eq/ 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) according to Sabia et al. (2020), 3.25 kg CO2 eq/1 kg of FPCM (Vagnoni and Franca 2018), between 2.61 and 5.17 kg CO2 eq/1 kg of FPCM (Batalla et al. 2015) and from 1.77 to 4.09 kg CO2 eq/ kg of milk (Escribano et al. 2020). Sabia et al. (2020) stated that enteric emissions are the major contributor to the carbon footprint, while Batalla et al. (2015) showed that more intensive farms have lower carbon footprint values than more traditional farms, but when soil carbon sequestration is included there are no statistically significant difference between groups. The hotspots identified by Vagnoni and Franca (2018) are methane enteric emissions and imported soybean meal use; however, their study did not account carbon sequestration. Escribano et al. (2020) concluded that enteric fermentation, feed manufacture and transport are mainly responsible for the carbon footprint of sheep's milk and that in extensive farms the carbon sequestration by the soil is significant and contributes significantly to reduction of greenhouse gases emission. Mondello et al. (2018) investigated the environ- mental burden of traditional Italian sheep cheese (Tuscan ""Pecorino"") production and proposed some improvement like: the use of an extensive breeding system and a pho- tovoltaic system for environmental impacts reduction. The environmental impacts associated with production of a traditional Portuguese sheep cheese (""Beira Baixa"") were determined by Nunes et al. (2020), while Vagnoni et al. (2017) compared the environmental profile of two Italian sheep cheese: ""Pecorino di Osilo"" a semi-artisanal typical cheese and ""Pecorino Romano"" cheese produced at indus- trial level. Functional units chosen by these authors were: 1 kg of sheep cheese (Mondello et al. 2018; Vagnoni et al. 2017), 1 kg of sheep milk and 1 kg of sheep cheese (Nunes et al. 2020). The steps considered in the system boundaries by Mondello et al. (2018) were breeding, milk transport and cheese production, while Vagnoni et al. (2017) considered for the evaluation beside production, collection of milk and cheese production, also the cheese distribution step. Nunes et al. (2020) included in the system boundaries milk produc- tion stage divided in three sub-stages (concentrated feed pro- duction, fodder production and milk production) and sheep cheese production. Mondello et al. (2018) and Vagnoni et al. (2017) used SimaPro software for determination of environ- mental impacts associated with production of sheep cheese, while Nunes et al. (2020) chosen GaBi software. Even though the authors used the same software, they chose dif- ferent methods for evaluation; for example, Mondello et al. (2018) used ReCiPe midpoint H method and considered all 18 impact categories included in this method in order to achieve a higher level of detail, while Vagnoni et al. (2017)"|"chose IPCC especially for the carbon footprint estimates and CML-IA version 3.3 for others categories (abiotic deple- tion, human toxicity, photochemical oxidation, acidification, eutrophication and impact categories regarding the environ- mental ecotoxicity). The hotspots identified by Mondello et al. (2018) and Vagnoni et al. (2017) were especially feed production and electricity consumption. Wastewater treat- ment was indicated by Mondello et al. (2018), also, as one of the hotspots. They proposed some performance improve- ment regarding the feed used, or forage species and also to change the conventional electricity source with green electricity sources. Vagnoni et al. (2017) even developed and evaluated four scenarios with different percentages of electricity from photovoltaic or wind power sources and con- cluded that the results are not statistically different. Nunes et al. (2020) compared their results with those obtained in other studies and concluded that milk production phase is the large contributor to climate change, terrestrial acidifica- tion and marine eutrophication. In Romania, there is a great tradition and experience for sheep production (Ilisiu et al. 2012). In 2020, there were approximately 10.5 million sheep in the country (Statista 2020). According to Juler (2014) about half of all sheep in Romania are kept for milk (turned into cheese) rather than for meat. The milk production and composition varies, depending on the breed of sheep: between 100 and 140 L (7.7% fat, 6.0% proteins, 16.65% total solids) for ""Turcana"" (the most important breed in Romania with 65% of sheep stock), and 70-90 L (7.0% fat, 6.5% proteins, 15.02% total solids) for ""Tigaie"" (Lujerdean et al. 2009). Most of the milk production is processed artisanal in small factories (Ilisiu et al. 2012), and according to Muregan et al. (2021), the cheese production in Romania has increased in recent years due to the development of small local factories. In Suceava county, the second largest county in Romania (8553.5 square kilometres), with a mountain area of 53%, plateau area 30% and meadow area 17%, the pastoral potential is high, and animal breeding is the preferential agricultural activity and an ancient occupation of the inhabitants (Suceava 2022). According to Grodea (2016), the highest concentration of the processing dairy factories approved for intra-community trade was found in the Suceava county. One typical Roma- nian cheese, with a specific recipe and technological manu- facturing process presented in the materials and methods section, was selected for this study. The chosen cheese is a traditional product, namely ""cas"", and from the ""cas"" whey is obtained ""urda"", a product relatively rich in pro- tein and low in fat. The characteristics of mountain ""cas"" according to ROMONTANA (2016), for 100 g, are: average nutritional value 1300 kj-300 kcal, dry matter 50%, 20 g protein, 28 g lipids, 0 g carbohydrates and 4.5 pH. Validity is 30 days, with storage between and + 6 °C. ""Urda"" is made up of milk lactalbumin, which remains in the whey"

after coagulation of casein. The sweet whey is heated to 85-90 acid whey (12%) is added, and after precipitation of lactalbumin and removal of the whey, it is placed in thick cloth bags to drain. The "urda" is white, with a soft, buttery consistency, sweet taste and the smell of boiled milk, and has the shape of the bags in which it was drained. It has a soft, crumbly texture. From 10-12 L of whey usually results 1 kg of "urda". The characteristics of the mountain "urda" for 100 g are as follows: 190 kcal average nutritional value, 40% dry matter, 18 g protein, 8 g lipids, 5 g carbohydrates and pH 5.5 (ROMONTANA 2016). Validity is 4 days as such and 8 days in vacuum packed, with storage between + 4 and °C. An important aspect of environmental protection is that the whey is not discharged in the form of concentrated wastewater, but is used to obtain another type of cheese. "Urda" cheese production is practiced in mountainous areas in Romania (Iligiu et al. 2012), Montenegro (Bojanic Raso- vic et al. 2017), Macedonia (Teneva-Angelova et al. 2018) and Greece (Pappa et al. 2020). Sheep's milk was obtained from sheep that spend the summer grazing in the Carpathian Mountains, and in winter they go down to the shelters in the valley. The type of energy consumed is one of the most important sources of environmental impacts associated with production of cheese. In 2015, the main energy resource in Romania was natural gas (29% in the primary energy mix) followed by crude oil (27%). However, Romania is rich in renewable energy resources as: wind, photovoltaic energy, biomass, hydropower, geothermal potential (Dragomir et al. 2016; Cîrstea et al. 2018; Vrínceanu et al. 2022). It has the highest potential in Southeast Europe in the field of wind energy, with high energy potential in the littoral and coastal areas, but also in alpine areas with plateaus and mountain valleys. Benefiting from 210 sunny days a year, Romania has a wide solar potential spread all over the country. Wind energy is produced in Romania starting from 2007 (annual wind potential approximately 23 TWh), while until 2017 photovoltaic power stations or photovoltaic (PV) parks with a combined installed capacity of 4871.66 MW were built. In north-east of the country, there are 26 PV farms with 28.15 MW installed capacity (Cîrstea et al. 2018). Accord- ing to Cîrstea et al. (2018) production of biogas and bio- fuels in Romania has high potential and the issue of waste management can be solved by energy and biogas produc- tion. Biogas for heat and power can be produced through anaerobic digestion of biomass waste. Achinas et al. (2018) demonstrated that biogas can be obtained from sheep manure co-digestion with cow dung, while Cestonaro et al. (2015) produced biogas and biofertilizer through anaerobic co-digestion of sheep bedding with cow manure. Alkhamis et al. (2021) indicated that sheep manure production can be 0.42 kg manure/head/day and estimated that 258 L biogas/ kg manure can be obtained at standard temperature and pres- sure conditions.

In this context, the present study provides valuable infor- mation for dairy industry, especially for those who want to reduce the environmental impacts associated with cheese production. This study is based on real data used for deter- mination of hotspots, which can conduct to economic and environmental benefits, enlarge database information and increase knowledge about the environmental performance of mountain dairy systems. The study includes a sensitivity analysis for transportation of sheep cheese produced in the mountain area and three scenarios which propose the uti- lization of energy from renewable sources in exchange for energy consumption from conventional sources.
8: <header>Introduction </header>

Worldwide, there is a constant increase in demand for dairy products (Canellada et al. 2018; Ghinea and Leahu 2020; Pulina et al. 2018; Rivas et al. 2019). Sheep milk has major nutritional value compared to cow and goat milks, due to high levels of protein, fat and calcium, which makes this type of milk excellent for cheese production (Balthazar et al. 2017). Mohapatra et al. (2019) indicated that sheep milk contains between 4.50 and 6.60% proteins, 6.99% fat, 3.90-4.90% lactose and 82% water, while Balthazar et al.

(2017) reported a content of 5.5% proteins, 5.9% fat, 4.8% lactose and 82.9% water. Most abundant protein from sheep milk is casein (4.2 to 5.2 g/100 g, representing around 80% of the total milk protein), which is very important in cheese production (Balthazar et al. 2017; Moatsou and Sakkas 2019; Park et al. 2007). The chemical composition of sheep milk depends on several factors like individuals, animal age, nutrition, lactation, season, environmental temperature, management, treatment and others (Balthazar et al. 2017; Mohapatra et al. 2019).

In 2018, the world production of sheep milk was esti- mated by FAOSTAT (2020) at 10,225,238 tonnes. In EU28, in the same year the fresh sheep milk production was regis- tered at 2,844,990 tonnes, while in Romania, 626,145 tonnes of sheep milk was obtained. Yearly, an average of 89 L of milk per ewe is obtained in Romania, much lower, compara- tive with 106 L of milk per ewe in Greece or 243 L milk per ewe in Spain (Pulina et al. 2018). At European level, in 2019, Greece was the main producer of sheep milk, followed by
Responsible Editor: Philippe Loubet
Cristina Ghinea cristina.ghinea@fia.usv.ro; cbghinea@yahoo.com 1 Faculty of Food Engineering, Stefan Cel Mare University of Suceava, 13 Universitatii Street, 720229 Suceava, Romania

Spain, Italy, Romania, France, Bulgaria and Portugal. From Fig. 1a, it can be observed that the total quantity of sheep milk produced in Romania decreased from 673,477 tonnes in 2014 to 425,500 tonnes in 2019. At global level, 726,421 tonnes of sheep cheese were produced in 2018, from which 402,743 tonnes of cheese from sheep milk were obtained only in the EU28 (FAOSTAT 2020). Approximately, 24,025 tonnes of sheep cheese were obtained in Romania, in 2018. According to FAOSTAT (2020), sheep cheese production in Romania has increased in recent years (Fig. 1b). In Roma- nia, cheese consumption is estimated at 5 kg /year per per- son. According to FRD Center (2016), 86% from Romanian consumers prefers cheese from cow milk, 53% cheese from sheep milk and 20% cheese from goat milk.

As all human activities, milk processing and dairy indus- try generate environmental impacts. Producers, consumers and other stakeholders believe that it is necessary to reduce these environmental impacts and increase sustainability (Cabral et al. 2020). The life cycle of products can be evalu- ated from environmental point of view by using life cycle

assessment (LCA); for example, the environmental impacts of a Romanian pork meat product (with pig farm, slaugh- terhouse and meat processing, transport and wastewater treatment, incineration of organic waste included in the system boundaries) were investigated by Ghinea and Leahu (2018), while the environmental footprints of a Romanian yogurt type (with activities such as milk processing, trans- port, solid waste and wastewater treatments included in the system boundaries) were determined by Ghinea and Leahu (2020). Different authors had used LCA for environmental impact assessment associated with sheep milk and cheese production (Mondello et al. 2018; Nunes et al. 2020; Vag- noni et al. 2017). The contribution of sheep milk production to the climate change was investigated by Sabia et al. (2020) considering four dairy sheep Italian family farms located in Basilicata Region, while Vagnoni and Franca (2018) investigated the contribution of two different production systems adopted to the same dairy sheep farm located in north-western Sardinia. Also, Batalla et al. (2015) calculated the carbon footprint of 12 individual Spanish dairy sheep

The milk production and composition varies, depending on the breed of sheep: between 100 and 140 L (7.7% fat, 6.0% proteins, 16.65% total solids) for ""Turcana"" (the most important breed in Romania with 65% of sheep stock), and 70-90 L (7.0% fat, 6.5% proteins, 15.02% total solids) for ""Tigaie"" (Lujerdean et al. 2009). Most of the milk production is processed artisanal in small factories (Ilisiu et al. 2012), and according to Muregan et al. (2021), the cheese production in Romania has increased in recent years due to the development of small local factories. In Suceava county, the second largest county in Romania (8553.5 square kilometres), with a mountain area of 53%, plateau area 30% and meadow area 17%, the pastoral potential is high, and animal breeding is the preferential agricultural activity and an ancient occupation of the inhabitants (Suceava 2022). According to Grodea (2016), the highest concentration of the processing dairy factories approved for intra-community trade was found in the Suceava county. One typical Roma- nian cheese, with a specific recipe and technological manu- facturing process presented in the materials and methods section, was selected for this study. The chosen cheese is a traditional product, namely ""cas"", and from the ""cas"" whey is obtained ""urda"", a product relatively rich in pro- tein and low in fat. The characteristics of mountain ""cas"" according to ROMONTANA (2016), for 100 g, are: average nutritional value 1300 kj-300 kcal, dry matter 50%, 20 g protein, 28 g lipids, 0 g carbohydrates and 4.5 pH. Validity is 30 days, with storage between and + 6 °C. ""Urda"" is made up of milk lactalbumin, which remains in the whey"

after coagulation of casein. The sweet whey is heated to 85-90 acid whey (12%) is added, and after precipitation of lactalbumin and removal of the whey, it is placed in thick cloth bags to drain. The "urda" is white, with a soft, buttery consistency, sweet taste and the smell of boiled milk, and has the shape of the bags in which it was drained. It has a soft, crumbly texture. From 10-12 L of whey usually results 1 kg of "urda". The characteristics of the mountain "urda" for 100 g are as follows: 190 kcal average nutritional value, 40% dry matter, 18 g protein, 8 g lipids, 5 g carbohydrates and pH 5.5 (ROMONTANA 2016). Validity is 4 days as such and 8 days in vacuum packed, with storage between + 4 and °C. An important aspect of environmental protection is that the whey is not discharged in the form of concentrated wastewater, but is used to obtain another type of cheese. "Urda" cheese production is practiced in mountainous areas in Romania (Iligiu et al. 2012), Montenegro (Bojanic Raso- vic et al. 2017), Macedonia (Teneva-Angelova et al. 2018) and Greece (Pappa et al. 2020). Sheep's milk was obtained from sheep that spend the summer grazing in the Carpathian Mountains, and in winter they go down to the shelters in the valley. The type of energy consumed is one of the most important sources of environmental impacts associated with production of cheese. In 2015, the main energy resource in Romania was natural gas (29% in the primary energy mix) followed by crude oil (27%). However, Romania is rich in renewable energy resources as: wind, photovoltaic energy, biomass, hydropower, geothermal potential (Dragomir et al. 2016; Cîrstea et al. 2018; Vrínceanu et al. 2022). It has the highest potential in Southeast Europe in the field of wind energy, with high energy potential in the littoral and coastal areas, but also in alpine areas with plateaus and mountain valleys. Benefiting from 210 sunny days a year, Romania has a wide solar potential spread all over the country. Wind energy is produced in Romania starting from 2007 (annual wind potential approximately 23 TWh), while until 2017 photovoltaic power stations or photovoltaic (PV) parks with a combined installed capacity of 4871.66 MW were built. In north-east of the country, there are 26 PV farms with 28.15 MW installed capacity (Cîrstea et al. 2018). Accord- ing to Cîrstea et al. (2018) production of biogas and bio- fuels in Romania has high potential and the issue of waste management can be solved by energy and biogas produc- tion. Biogas for heat and power can be produced through anaerobic digestion of biomass waste. Achinas et al. (2018) demonstrated that biogas can be obtained from sheep manure co-digestion with cow dung, while Cestonaro et al. (2015) produced biogas and biofertilizer through anaerobic co-digestion of sheep bedding with cow manure. Alkhamis et al. (2021) indicated that sheep manure production can be 0.42 kg manure/head/day and estimated that 258 L biogas/ kg manure can be obtained at standard temperature and pres- sure conditions.

In this context, the present study provides valuable infor- mation for dairy industry, especially for those who want to reduce the environmental impacts associated with cheese production. This study is based on real data used for deter- mination of hotspots, which can conduct to economic and environmental benefits, enlarge database information and increase knowledge about the environmental performance of mountain dairy systems. The study includes a sensitivity analysis for transportation of sheep cheese produced in the mountain area and three scenarios which propose the uti- lization of energy from renewable sources in exchange for energy consumption from conventional sources.
10: This study was performed for a small cheese factory located in Eastern Romanian Carpathians. Situated in the rural area of Suceava, this factory is a family business inherited from father to son. The nearest village is located 10 km from the factory site. Machines used are new, high performance, with digital panels. There are two new refrigeration rooms for fresh ones and a freezing room if needed. There is also a finished goods warehouse in a separate pavilion, to which are added two freezing-refrigeration chambers. The factory also has three fully equipped laboratories, physico-chemical, microbio- logical and one for the reception of milk-the raw material. This factory produces mainly cheese from sheep milk. The sheep graze in the mountains, only in winter they retreat to the sheepfold. The technological manufacturing process (Fig. 2) involves the following operations (Banu 2009):

<list>- Qualitative and quantitative reception of sheep's milk, which must have an acidity of 23-26°T; 
- Cleaning of the milk by means of filters or by centrifugal means, the purified milk being used as such or normal- ized, in the raw state or pasteurized and cooled (depend- ing on the technical equipment); 
- Normalization is the operation of bringing the milk to the percentage of fat provided in the quality norms of the sheep's milk cheese product, and this is done with the addition of skimmed milk; 
- The milk is pasteurized at 63-65 °C for 20-30 min. The pasteurized milk is cooled to 31-33 °C; 
Maturation of milk (optional), which is done with a selected lactic bacteria production culture composed of: Str. lactis, Str. Thermophilus and L.casei; 
- The preparation of milk for coagulation consists in add- ing calcium chloride (CaCl2) and lactic acid bacteria cultures to pasteurized and cooled milk. In order to obtain a firm curd, 10-15 g of CaCl2 can be added to 100 L of milk (when using sheep's milk towards the end of lactation can be added 10-20 CaCl2 /100 L milk).</list>

Reception|Reception||Centrifugal cleaning||Normalization||Homogenization||Pasteurization|Pasteurization Reception|Reception||Centrifugal cleaning||Normalization||Homogenization||Pasteurization|Pasteurization |||||||||| Curd processing|Curd processing||Clotting||Inoculation||Preparation for coagulation|Preparation for coagulation|Preparation for coagulation|Preparation for coagulation |||||||||| Forming pressing and|Forming pressing and||Salting||Washing and rinsing||Cheese packaging||maturation Cheese|maturation Cheese
Fig. 2 Steps for Romanian sheep cheese production
The milk is curdled at a temperature between 31 and 33 °C for 60 and 70 min;

<list>Curd processing consists of: returning the curd from the surface with the scaffold; cutting the curd with a harp into prismatic columns with a side of 2-3 cm; cutting columns into cubes with side 2-3 cm; rest for 5-10 min and drain the whey released. The curd is passed and processed on the crest (diagonal cloth binding; self- pressing for 20-30 min; opening the cloth, breaking the edge of the curd and tying the cloth again; self-pressing 30 min), and then, the curd is pressed (unwinding the cloth and cheese uniformity; tightening the cloth in the form of an envelope; laying a cloth lid over the cheese; the actual pressing of the cheese from a frame of the crest with 20 kgf for 10-15 min, then increasing the pressing force up to 40 kgf.). The pressing time is 120-150 min and is considered finished when the whey drips into large, sparse, clear drops; 
Forming of cheese imply cutting the cheese mass from a compartment into square pieces with a side of 11 cm, the cut blocks being left on the crest for 15-20 min to drain the rest of the whey; 
Wet salting consists in introducing the cheese blocks placed in plastic shuttles in a basin with brine of 20-22% concentration, acidity 20-30°T and tempera- ture of 14-18 °C. At the interval of 6-7 h, the shut- tles are removed from the basin, the blocks are turned, sprinkled with salt (10-15 g per block), and the shut- tles are introduced again into the brine basin. The total duration of the salting is 15-18 h; 
For maturation, sheep cheese is passed into rooms with a temperature of 14-16 °C, and the process takes about 20 days;</list>

<list>The packaging of sheep cheese is made in polyethylene foils, printed in 1-6 colours, with the inscriptions of the methodological norms regarding food labelling. The raw material from which the film is made is low density polyethylene, anti-blocking and non-slip additives, colour concentrate and printing inks, all of which must have the sanitary approval for use in the food industry; 
Storage is performed in cold storage at 4-8 °C, storage time being 12 months. In warehouses arranged in base- ments, the temperature must not exceed 12 °C, the stor- age period being 4 months.</list>

Life cycle assessment (LCA) was used for determination of environmental impacts associated with sheep cheese pro- duction and distribution. LCA is a standardized tool with four steps: goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment and interpretation (ISO - International Organization for Standardization 14040, 2006a; b).

15: The main goal of this study is to determine the environmen- tal impacts associated with the sheep cheese production, to investigate the energy production contribution and to analyse the environmental burden when the cheese is transported for sale from the mountains area in different cities of the coun- try. The product system included: function-cheese produc- tion from sheep milk, functional unit-1 kg of cheese, sys- tem boundaries (Fig. 3). Retail and consumption phases were not considered, since the goal of this study was focused more on process, type of electricity and transportation, aspects especially required by the dairy factory. Regarding the data

0: quality, multiple sources of input/output flows were used (direct measurement, personal interviews (primary data), bibliographic sources (secondary data), background data (as Romanian energy generation) from GaBi database. In data quality requirements were included measured data (direct measured), verified data (not measured directly), estimated data (were obtained by estimates of the factory employees) and calculated data (were obtained by applying a mass bal- ance in the system) (Table 1). Assumptions and limitations: environmental impacts due to infrastructure, production of machinery, and devices used, were not considered.

The factory produces in addition to "cas" cheese, "urda" cheese from whey, which means environmental benefits. In this case, when co-product is obtained, allocation usually decreases the environmental impacts associated with the cheese production (Canellada et al. 2018; González-García et al. 2013a). ISO 14044 (2006b) provides allocation guid- ance, and the first indication is: avoid allocation whenever possible by dividing the unit processes into sub-processes or using the system extension to include co-products; when allocation cannot be avoided, identification of physical relationships between the multiple products and functions must be performed, in order to partition the environmental impacts; if a physical relationship cannot be established, another relationship between products and co-products should be identified, like economic value. According to

Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2012), no allocation is needed when whey is as a waste (this is not the case in the present study); also the allocation can be avoided by subdividing the unit processes that are unique to co-products obtained, but the curd/whey separation reflects a chemical separation of milk and it seems that is not possible to avoid allocation. Approxi- mately 73% from milk used for cheese production represents the whey; therefore, the results of the cheese impacts on the environment can be underestimated due to the mass alloca- tion which will transfer the environmental impacts to whey. An economic approach will be more suitable in this case; therefore, based on the prices existent on the Romanian mar- ket, it was assumed 6 euro/kg of "cas" cheese and 4 euro/kg of cheese from whey, resulting an allocation factor of 80% for cheese production and 10% for cheese from whey. For sensitivity analysis, four allocation factors were considered starting from 70 to 100% of cheese production.
1: quality, multiple sources of input/output flows were used (direct measurement, personal interviews (primary data), bibliographic sources (secondary data), background data (as Romanian energy generation) from GaBi database. In data quality requirements were included measured data (direct measured), verified data (not measured directly), estimated data (were obtained by estimates of the factory employees) and calculated data (were obtained by applying a mass bal- ance in the system) (Table 1). Assumptions and limitations: environmental impacts due to infrastructure, production of machinery, and devices used, were not considered.

The factory produces in addition to "cas" cheese, "urda" cheese from whey, which means environmental benefits. In this case, when co-product is obtained, allocation usually decreases the environmental impacts associated with the cheese production (Canellada et al. 2018; González-García et al. 2013a). ISO 14044 (2006b) provides allocation guid- ance, and the first indication is: avoid allocation whenever possible by dividing the unit processes into sub-processes or using the system extension to include co-products; when allocation cannot be avoided, identification of physical relationships between the multiple products and functions must be performed, in order to partition the environmental impacts; if a physical relationship cannot be established, another relationship between products and co-products should be identified, like economic value. According to

Aguirre-Villegas et al. (2012), no allocation is needed when whey is as a waste (this is not the case in the present study); also the allocation can be avoided by subdividing the unit processes that are unique to co-products obtained, but the curd/whey separation reflects a chemical separation of milk and it seems that is not possible to avoid allocation. Approxi- mately 73% from milk used for cheese production represents the whey; therefore, the results of the cheese impacts on the environment can be underestimated due to the mass alloca- tion which will transfer the environmental impacts to whey. An economic approach will be more suitable in this case; therefore, based on the prices existent on the Romanian mar- ket, it was assumed 6 euro/kg of "cas" cheese and 4 euro/kg of cheese from whey, resulting an allocation factor of 80% for cheese production and 10% for cheese from whey. For sensitivity analysis, four allocation factors were considered starting from 70 to 100% of cheese production.

a-User-defined process (RO - Romania). b-Process already existing in the database (EU-28; GLO-Global). Measured data were direct measured; verified data were not measured directly; estimated data were obtained by estimates of the factory employees; calculated data were obtained by applying a mass balance in the system

technological losses, packaging waste and wastewater, were obtained from the factory and were further processed and reported to the functional unit. Data like emissions from burning of 1 kg of diesel were obtained from different stud- ies (Ghinea et al. 2012; Recycled Organics Unit 2003). Ecoinvent and GaBi databases were used for obtaining data about the electricity generation with Romanian profile,

diesel production, water process and salt production (GaBi 2020; Wernet et al. 2016). Also, data regarding wastewater treatment and landfilling were adapted upon data from GaBi database (GaBi 2020). Combustion emission and distribu- tion distances were calculated.
The sheep milk amount necessary for production of 1 kg of cheese is 4.56 kg received. 0.257 kg of technological

losses and 0.05 g of packaging waste are resulted from the technological process of one kg cheese production. Approximately 8.5 L of wastewater and 3.303 kg of whey are resulted from the production process. Whey is used to obtain another assortment of cheese. Emissions in air resulted from utilization of diesel (3.21 kg) were calculated and are pre- sented in Table 1. Electricity consumption for obtaining of 1 kg of sheep cheese was assumed to be 0.83 kWh. The dairy factory it does not use natural gas in the process, only electricity and diesel. Stages such as normalization, homog- enization, pasteurization, processing and pressing are the main energy consumers according to the representatives of the dairy factory. But it was found that the stages of packag- ing and storage/refrigeration also have a high consumption of electricity. Diesel is used for milk transportation from the mountain sheepfold to the factory. Also, diesel is consumed in some technological stages of sheep cheese production, in fuel installation for heat and hot water production. The water is used for animal nutrition, washing the milking equipment, collection and transport containers, cleaning in the dairy fac- tory. Information provided by factory representatives was on the whole plant and not on each process. Refrigerated trucks, owned by the company or by third parties, are used for cheese distribution. The company owns two euro 6 trucks: refrigerator/isotherm with capacity of 7.5 t. Truck 13 pallets, refrigerator and freezer + 20 °C, 2 rooms with partition wall, 7-cm-thick walls, 3-piston diesel engine + 380 V, interior dimensions: 5.15 2.35 m, in perfect working order. Three cheese distribution scenarios were considered in this study: transport scenario 1 (TS1): it involves covering the 112 km from the factory to Suceava city, 266 km pro- posed for TS2 (from the factory to Bacau city) and 469 km established for TS3 (from the factory to the capital-Bucha- rest). The transport scenarios were selected based on the contracts that the company has with various collaborators in order to sell the product. The scenarios were built to perform sensitivity analysis on transportation distances, in order to get an idea on the effect of transportation distance on the environmental impacts of sheep cheese supply. The same aspect related to transport was investigated by Uctug et al. (2019) for yoghurt supply.

technological losses, packaging waste and wastewater, were obtained from the factory and were further processed and reported to the functional unit. Data like emissions from burning of 1 kg of diesel were obtained from different stud- ies (Ghinea et al. 2012; Recycled Organics Unit 2003). Ecoinvent and GaBi databases were used for obtaining data about the electricity generation with Romanian profile,

diesel production, water process and salt production (GaBi 2020; Wernet et al. 2016). Also, data regarding wastewater treatment and landfilling were adapted upon data from GaBi database (GaBi 2020). Combustion emission and distribu- tion distances were calculated.
The sheep milk amount necessary for production of 1 kg of cheese is 4.56 kg received. 0.257 kg of technological

losses and 0.05 g of packaging waste are resulted from the technological process of one kg cheese production. Approximately 8.5 L of wastewater and 3.303 kg of whey are resulted from the production process. Whey is used to obtain another assortment of cheese. Emissions in air resulted from utilization of diesel (3.21 kg) were calculated and are pre- sented in Table 1. Electricity consumption for obtaining of 1 kg of sheep cheese was assumed to be 0.83 kWh. The dairy factory it does not use natural gas in the process, only electricity and diesel. Stages such as normalization, homog- enization, pasteurization, processing and pressing are the main energy consumers according to the representatives of the dairy factory. But it was found that the stages of packag- ing and storage/refrigeration also have a high consumption of electricity. Diesel is used for milk transportation from the mountain sheepfold to the factory. Also, diesel is consumed in some technological stages of sheep cheese production, in fuel installation for heat and hot water production. The water is used for animal nutrition, washing the milking equipment, collection and transport containers, cleaning in the dairy fac- tory. Information provided by factory representatives was on the whole plant and not on each process. Refrigerated trucks, owned by the company or by third parties, are used for cheese distribution. The company owns two euro 6 trucks: refrigerator/isotherm with capacity of 7.5 t. Truck 13 pallets, refrigerator and freezer + 20 °C, 2 rooms with partition wall, 7-cm-thick walls, 3-piston diesel engine + 380 V, interior dimensions: 5.15 2.35 m, in perfect working order. Three cheese distribution scenarios were considered in this study: transport scenario 1 (TS1): it involves covering the 112 km from the factory to Suceava city, 266 km pro- posed for TS2 (from the factory to Bacau city) and 469 km established for TS3 (from the factory to the capital-Bucha- rest). The transport scenarios were selected based on the contracts that the company has with various collaborators in order to sell the product. The scenarios were built to perform sensitivity analysis on transportation distances, in order to get an idea on the effect of transportation distance on the environmental impacts of sheep cheese supply. The same aspect related to transport was investigated by Uctug et al. (2019) for yoghurt supply.

9: GaBi software was used to determine the environmental impacts associated with production and distribution of sheep cheese. The impact assessment was performed considering two LCA methods included in the software: CML 2001-Janu- ary 2016 and ReCiPe 1.08 midpoint (H). According to Santos et al. (2017): "ReCiPe is an update of the CML method used in other similar studies that assessed dairy products". The

indicator scores are determined in a similar way as in the EI99 method. CML (Guinée et al. 2002) is one of the most com- plete and commonly used methods, while ReCiPe (Goedkoop et al. 2013) is an enriched and harmonized method. CML has a global vision of the impact, while ReCiPe is more applicable to Europe (Reyes et al. 2019). By analyzing these two methods, even if they have common impact categories, it was observed that the characterization factors and normalization references are different (Aymard and Botta-Genoulaz 2017). Impact cat- egories included in CML 2001-January 2016 method and con- sidered in this study were: abiotic depletion potential elements (ADP acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), global warming potential (GWP), human toxicity poten- tial (HTP) (PE International 2009). ReCiPe 1.08 midpoint (H) method includes various impact categories, from which the following categories were selected for this study: climate change (CC), fossil depletion (FD), freshwater eutrophica- tion (FET), particulate matter formation (PMF), photochemi- cal oxidant formation (POF), terrestrial acidification (TA) (GaBi 2020). A major contributor to climate change is the food system, with dairy products ranking second in terms of food-related greenhouse gas emissions, after meat products. It was demonstrated that dairy products have significant effect on water quality through eutrophication, acidification, bio- logical and chemical pollution. In addition to climate change and water pollution, the dairy sector contributes to air pollu- tion, land use change and soil degradation (Pope et al. 2021). The impact categories were chosen in this study based on the results of other studies evaluating the life cycle of dairy prod- ucts. For example, Djekic et al. (2014) considered the GWP, AP, EP, HT impact categories for evaluation of cheese, butter, yogurt, milk, and cream, while González-García et al. (2013a, b) demonstrated that milk production has the highest impact on AP, EP, GWP categories and cheese-making has highest environmental impacts on ADP e. Palmieri et al. (2017) con- sidered for milk and mozzarella cheese production several impact categories such as AD, abiotic depletion (fossil fuels) (AD f), GWP, ozone layer depletion (ODP), HT, freshwater and marine aquatic ecotoxicity (FW, ME), terrestrial ecotox- icity (TE), photochemical oxidation (PO), AP and EP. Also, Bava et al. (2018) considered the following impact categories: CC, ODP, PMF, POF, TA, FET, FD, marine eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity and mineral depletion for environmental evaluation of a traditional Italian cheese. Characterization and normalization were applied. The normalization factors were related to Europe reference.
