11: Characterization results obtained by applying CML 2001-January 2016 method showed that cheese technologi- cal process mainly contributes to acidification potential,

eutrophication potential and global warming potential due to the diesel used in some stages (Fig. 4). Raw milk pro- duction, sodium chloride, diesel and electricity account for 51.38%, 20.66%, 19.45% and 6.23% to the abiotic depletion potential elements. Results indicate that 52.10%, 36.37% and 7.58% of the global warming potential were due to the diesel used in technological process of cheese, raw milk production and diesel production. It was found that wastewater treat- ment contributes with the highest percentage (95.32%) to the human toxicity potential. The most important input for acidification potential is cheese production (87.55%). LCA results indicated that 53.64% and 39.65% of the eutrophi- cation potential were derived from cheese and raw milk productions.

The results obtained by applying ReCiPe 1.08 mid- point (H) method, for determination of environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese production, are illustrated in Fig. 5. The technological process of cheese produc- tion is responsible with 82.36% for the climate change impact category, followed by diesel production (11.70%),

electricity production (4.87%) and landfilling with 1.07%. Electricity mix production and burning of fossil fuels were also indicated by Nunes et al. (2020) as contribu- tors to climate change. Regarding the fossil depletion, it was observed that diesel production is the main con- tributor to this impact category with 95.46%. Wastewater treatment (43.58%), raw milk production (37.52%), diesel production (12.34%) and landfilling (6.30%) contribute to freshwater eutrophication. In terms of the particu- late matter formation impact category, it was observed that raw milk production has the largest contribution (71.04%), followed by cheese production (21.56%), die- sel production with a contribution of 4.62%, electricity with 3.02% and the others with a contribution below 1%. Diesel production (51.12%), cheese technological process (32.16%), electricity production (14.39%) and landfilling (1.07%) contributes to photochemical oxidant formation. Results indicates that cheese technological process, die- sel production and electricity production contribute with 60.09%, 22.98% and 16.53% to terrestrial acidification.

Fig. 4 CML 2001- Jan. 2016 evaluation method charac- terisation results (in %) for each impact categories (ADP e-abiotic depletion potential elements, AP - acidification potential, EP - eutrophica- tion potential, GWP - global warming potential, HTP- human toxicity potential)

Fig. 5 Environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese produc- tion obtained by using ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) method (characterisation results, in %) (CC-climate change, FD-fos- sil depletion, FET-freshwater eutrophication, PMF-par- ticulate matter formation, POF- photochemical oxidant formation, TA-terrestrial acidification)

Normalization results are presented in Fig. 6. Accord- ing to Fig. 6a, production of 1 kg sheep cheese mainly contributes to human toxicity potential. The contribu- tion of the sheep cheese production (SCP) process for the impact categories decreases in the following order: HTP > AP > GWP > EP. Results showed that distribution of sheep cheese contributes mainly to AP and GWP. It was observed that contribution of this stage to the inves- tigated impact categories decreases in the following order: AP > GWP > EP > HTP. Therefore, transportation contrib- utes more significantly to AP, GWP and EP, than to the other impact categories to which it has lower contributions. It was determined that from the total contribution of SCP and TS to AP: in the case of the first scenario, 35% contributes SCP and 65% TS1, for the second scenario the contribution of the production process decreased and contribution of transport (TS2) increased to 85%, while for the last scenario contribu- tion of transport (TS3) increased to 89%. The same situation

was observed for EP. In the case of GWP, SCP contributes with 47% and TS1 with 53%, SCP contributes with 24% and TS2 with 76% and finally SCP contributes with 18% and TS3 with 82%. As expected, increasing the distance for sheep cheese distribution leads to increased environmental impact, more specifically for AP, GWP and EP, due to the emissions from diesel combustion.

From Fig. 6b, it can be observed that SCP contributes especially to FET, followed by the contribution to CC, FD and others impact categories. Transport phase contribution to the investigated impact categories decreases in the fol- lowing order CC > PMF > TA > POF. The total contribution of SCP and TS to CC differs depending on the transport scenario: 71% SCP and 29% TS1; 46% SCP and 54% TS2; 37% SCP and 63% TS3. It was also observed that also for the other impact categories mentioned above (PMF, TA and POF) the contribution of distribution stage increases with increasing transport distances (Fig. 6b).

Fig. 6 a) CML2001- Jan. 2016 and b) ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) normalisation results (Europe reference) related to 1 kg of sheep cheese produc- tion (SCP) and transported (considering different trans- port scenarios (TS): 112 km, 266 km, 469 km), PE - person equivalents
According to different LCA studies, the electricity con-

sumption can vary significantly, depending on the type of cheese, process conditions and the plant age and size, from 0.36 kWh kg- of cheese (Berlin 2002) to 1.12 kWh kg-1 of cheese (Vagnoni et al. 2017). Ladha-Sabur et al. (2019) calculated an average of 0.30 kWh kg of cheese, while González-García et al. (2013b) established that electricity consumption can be 0.71 kWh kg-1 of cheese. The energy consumption for cheese production in this study was esti- mated at 0.83 kWh kg of cheese and falls within the val- ues found in the literature. Electricity grid mix in Romania, according to database used (GaBi 2018), included in 2014 the following energy sources: 29.36% hydro, 26.62% lignite, 17.78% nuclear, 12.34% natural gas and others. Although in 2014 it experienced an unprecedented development, the production based on unconventional sources (wind, pho- tovoltaic, biomass) had a share of 11.98% of the annual energy delivered in networks (ANRE 2015). According to Eurostat (2021), the price of electrical energy for non- household's medium size consumers varied in the last years from 0.0753 EUR/kwh in 2014 to 0.0890 EUR/kwh in 2020. Renewable energy sources are known as a major alternative to sustainable energy, by reducing greenhouse gas emis- sions and energy costs (Burloiu and Rusu 2021; Vrínceanu et al. 2022). There are a number of studies that verify the feasibility of photovoltaic systems, wind turbines in dairy farms (Bey et al. 2016; Nacer et al. 2016a, b), of the two integrated systems (PV/wind) (Nadjemi et al. 2017) or of the PV/biomass solar hybrid energy system (Blanchet et al. 2019). Dell'Anna and Menconi (2016) investigated techno- logical solutions (such as sloping photovoltaic (PV) grids on the roofs of buildings) that can meet the energy needs of small food producers in rural areas. Off grid box is an integrated technical device developed by an Italian group, and it may have six or more 250 Wp monocrystalline crystal- line solar modules connected in parallel to a secure protec- tion control panel. A solar inverter is connected to standard AC batteries, and the same inverter can transform energy from a wind turbine (500 W), attached to the off grid box structure (Dell'Anna and Menconi 2016). On the market can be found small turbines with horizontal axis and vertical axis which can be used in rural area and they can be cho- sen based on wind conditions and type of terrain (Messineo and Culotta 2012). Renewable energy can be obtained in the form of biogas through anaerobic digestion of a variety of organic waste streams. According to Traub et al. (2021), there is a highest interest among the small dairy producers to implement anaerobic digestion system. In the last years, Traub et al. (2021) developed modular small-farm anaerobic digestion technologies for a variety of food and agricultural waste types. The dairy site investigated in this study is situ- ated in a geographical area with an average annual wind speed of 3.99 m/s at 10 m height (NEWA 2022) and average|according to Paraschiv and Paraschiv (2018) the duration of sunlight ranges between 2.2 (in January) and 10.5 (in July) hours per day. Therefore, the main ecological and economic improve- ment of the dairy factory can be addressed to the use of energy from renewable sources. The processing capacity is currently reduced, but it plans to expand it and also takes into account the installation of solar panels for electricity production and of solar boilers. Considering this aspect, in order to provide data on the impact on the environment resulting from the production of different types of energy, three scenarios were developed: S1 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity production from solar photovoltaic, S2 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity production from wind, while S3 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity produc- tion from biogas. The GaBi databases referring to Romanian electrical energy were used: RO: power from biogas-alter- nating current (AC), mix of direct and combined heat and power plants (CHP), technology mix regarding firing and flue gas cleaning, 1 kV-60 kV; RO: power from photovolta- ics-AC, technology mix of CIS, CdTE, monocrystalline and multi-crystalline, 1 kV-601 RO: power from wind power- AC, technology mix of onshore and offshore, 1 kV-60 kV. The environmental impact of renewable energy production from these three different sources was determined. It was considered its use in different percentages 5, 10 and 20% in the process of obtaining cheese. In the sensitivity analysis, the following composition of energy sources were consid- ered for S1 95% grid mix and 5% solar energy, 90% grid mix and 10% solar energy, 80% grid mix and 20% solar energy; for 95% grid mix and 5% wind energy, 90% grid mix and 10% wind energy, 80% grid mix and 20% wind energy; and for S3 95% grid mix and 5% biogas energy, 90% grid mix and 10% biogas energy, 80% grid mix and 20% biogas energy. The panels selected for this study were considered to have a maximum power of 120 W (but there are also panels in 200-350 W) and can be installed at an angle of 30° to the horizontal. It has been assumed that the PV panels are used around 4-h of sun per day and 0.48 kWh per panel is obtained. There are needed 62 panels, and the maximum power of the photovoltaic system is around 7 kW; however, the system is producing more in summer months and less in winter. The number of panels can increase or decrease depending on the amount of energy we want to get. Most probably, the wind turbine will operate for 4 h/day from April to September and for 7 h/day between October and March, approximately 2007 h/year. It was assumed that for a wind turbine (12 m diameter and average wind speed m/s, under standard atmospheric conditions 1.225 kg/ m³) it can be obtained an annual energy production of 3556 kWh. In real conditions, the variability of the wind regime
solar radiation of 3.55 kWh/m2/day (EC 2022). In Romania,

and the characteristics of wind turbine will greatly influ- ence the output. The calculations were performed based on equations provided by Paraschiv and Paraschiv (2018). It was assumed that the biogas can be obtained from sheep manure and other biomass at dairy farm. There is potential for biogas production from manure; for example, Vac et al. (2013) investigated the greenhouse gas emissions from a farm with a capacity of 600 sheep, manure surface of 579 m², the measured emissions for sheep manure were: 0.83 t CH4 /year and 61.3 t CO2 /year. These emissions may not reach the atmosphere and be harnessed through anaerobic digestion. The manure production can be between 0.3 and 0.4 kg/sheep/day, depending on the animal breed and age. Future studies (technical and economic) will investigate these ways of obtaining electricity in more detail.
From the sensitivity analysis (Fig. 7), it can be seen that the scenario which includes the energy produced by the wind has smaller impacts on the environment, regarding ADPf,

GWP, compared to the other two scenarios, being followed by the scenario that includes the use of energy produced from photovoltaic panels. Also, the energy production sce- narios were investigated by applying Tukey pairwise com- parisons, considering data obtained for the two environmen- tal impact categories. From Table 2, it can be observed that the means for scenarios S3 and S2 are significantly different, while S1 is not significantly different than S3 and S2.

Regarding HTP, S2 has a lower contribution to this impact category being followed by S3 and S1. In the case of acidification potential, the largest contribution to this impact category has S3, followed by S1 and S2. The same was observed for EP. The results were shown to the cheese company, and an economic and technical analysis will be made to establish the optimal option for this company.
Changes from 70 to 100% in cheese production lead to an increase of 9.25% in AP, 7.36% in EP and 7.13% in GWP, and the ADPe and HTP categories are not influenced by
Fig. 7 Sensitivity analysis variation of environmental impacts: a) ADPf and b) GWP associated with energy produc- tion scenarios
Means that do not share a letter are significantly different

these changes, the contribution is the same regardless of the allocation factor (Fig. 8a). An increase of 10% in the allocation percentage (i.e. from 70 to 80, from 80 to 90 or from 90 to 100%) resulted in increases in AP (3.08%), EP (2.45%) and GWP (2.37%). If an allocation of 20% (from 70 to 90 or 80 to 100%) is taken into account, a maximum increase of 6.17% for AP is reached. When ReCiPe method is considered, it was observed that changes from 70 to 100% in cheese production lead to an increase of 5.22% in PMF and 2.58% in TA. CC and POF impact categories were less than 1% affected by these changes, while FD and FET were not influenced at all (Fig. 8b). PMF and TA increased with 1.74% and, respectively, 0.86% when an increase of 10% in the allocation percentage was performed. A maximum increase of 3.48% is obtained when allocation of 20% is considered.
12: Characterization results obtained by applying CML 2001-January 2016 method showed that cheese technologi- cal process mainly contributes to acidification potential,

eutrophication potential and global warming potential due to the diesel used in some stages (Fig. 4). Raw milk pro- duction, sodium chloride, diesel and electricity account for 51.38%, 20.66%, 19.45% and 6.23% to the abiotic depletion potential elements. Results indicate that 52.10%, 36.37% and 7.58% of the global warming potential were due to the diesel used in technological process of cheese, raw milk production and diesel production. It was found that wastewater treat- ment contributes with the highest percentage (95.32%) to the human toxicity potential. The most important input for acidification potential is cheese production (87.55%). LCA results indicated that 53.64% and 39.65% of the eutrophi- cation potential were derived from cheese and raw milk productions.

The results obtained by applying ReCiPe 1.08 mid- point (H) method, for determination of environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese production, are illustrated in Fig. 5. The technological process of cheese produc- tion is responsible with 82.36% for the climate change impact category, followed by diesel production (11.70%),

electricity production (4.87%) and landfilling with 1.07%. Electricity mix production and burning of fossil fuels were also indicated by Nunes et al. (2020) as contribu- tors to climate change. Regarding the fossil depletion, it was observed that diesel production is the main con- tributor to this impact category with 95.46%. Wastewater treatment (43.58%), raw milk production (37.52%), diesel production (12.34%) and landfilling (6.30%) contribute to freshwater eutrophication. In terms of the particu- late matter formation impact category, it was observed that raw milk production has the largest contribution (71.04%), followed by cheese production (21.56%), die- sel production with a contribution of 4.62%, electricity with 3.02% and the others with a contribution below 1%. Diesel production (51.12%), cheese technological process (32.16%), electricity production (14.39%) and landfilling (1.07%) contributes to photochemical oxidant formation. Results indicates that cheese technological process, die- sel production and electricity production contribute with 60.09%, 22.98% and 16.53% to terrestrial acidification.

Fig. 4 CML 2001- Jan. 2016 evaluation method charac- terisation results (in %) for each impact categories (ADP e-abiotic depletion potential elements, AP - acidification potential, EP - eutrophica- tion potential, GWP - global warming potential, HTP- human toxicity potential)

Fig. 5 Environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese produc- tion obtained by using ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) method (characterisation results, in %) (CC-climate change, FD-fos- sil depletion, FET-freshwater eutrophication, PMF-par- ticulate matter formation, POF- photochemical oxidant formation, TA-terrestrial acidification)

Normalization results are presented in Fig. 6. Accord- ing to Fig. 6a, production of 1 kg sheep cheese mainly contributes to human toxicity potential. The contribu- tion of the sheep cheese production (SCP) process for the impact categories decreases in the following order: HTP > AP > GWP > EP. Results showed that distribution of sheep cheese contributes mainly to AP and GWP. It was observed that contribution of this stage to the inves- tigated impact categories decreases in the following order: AP > GWP > EP > HTP. Therefore, transportation contrib- utes more significantly to AP, GWP and EP, than to the other impact categories to which it has lower contributions. It was determined that from the total contribution of SCP and TS to AP: in the case of the first scenario, 35% contributes SCP and 65% TS1, for the second scenario the contribution of the production process decreased and contribution of transport (TS2) increased to 85%, while for the last scenario contribu- tion of transport (TS3) increased to 89%. The same situation

was observed for EP. In the case of GWP, SCP contributes with 47% and TS1 with 53%, SCP contributes with 24% and TS2 with 76% and finally SCP contributes with 18% and TS3 with 82%. As expected, increasing the distance for sheep cheese distribution leads to increased environmental impact, more specifically for AP, GWP and EP, due to the emissions from diesel combustion.

From Fig. 6b, it can be observed that SCP contributes especially to FET, followed by the contribution to CC, FD and others impact categories. Transport phase contribution to the investigated impact categories decreases in the fol- lowing order CC > PMF > TA > POF. The total contribution of SCP and TS to CC differs depending on the transport scenario: 71% SCP and 29% TS1; 46% SCP and 54% TS2; 37% SCP and 63% TS3. It was also observed that also for the other impact categories mentioned above (PMF, TA and POF) the contribution of distribution stage increases with increasing transport distances (Fig. 6b).

Fig. 6 a) CML2001- Jan. 2016 and b) ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) normalisation results (Europe reference) related to 1 kg of sheep cheese produc- tion (SCP) and transported (considering different trans- port scenarios (TS): 112 km, 266 km, 469 km), PE - person equivalents
According to different LCA studies, the electricity con-

sumption can vary significantly, depending on the type of cheese, process conditions and the plant age and size, from 0.36 kWh kg- of cheese (Berlin 2002) to 1.12 kWh kg-1 of cheese (Vagnoni et al. 2017). Ladha-Sabur et al. (2019) calculated an average of 0.30 kWh kg of cheese, while González-García et al. (2013b) established that electricity consumption can be 0.71 kWh kg-1 of cheese. The energy consumption for cheese production in this study was esti- mated at 0.83 kWh kg of cheese and falls within the val- ues found in the literature. Electricity grid mix in Romania, according to database used (GaBi 2018), included in 2014 the following energy sources: 29.36% hydro, 26.62% lignite, 17.78% nuclear, 12.34% natural gas and others. Although in 2014 it experienced an unprecedented development, the production based on unconventional sources (wind, pho- tovoltaic, biomass) had a share of 11.98% of the annual energy delivered in networks (ANRE 2015). According to Eurostat (2021), the price of electrical energy for non- household's medium size consumers varied in the last years from 0.0753 EUR/kwh in 2014 to 0.0890 EUR/kwh in 2020. Renewable energy sources are known as a major alternative to sustainable energy, by reducing greenhouse gas emis- sions and energy costs (Burloiu and Rusu 2021; Vrínceanu et al. 2022). There are a number of studies that verify the feasibility of photovoltaic systems, wind turbines in dairy farms (Bey et al. 2016; Nacer et al. 2016a, b), of the two integrated systems (PV/wind) (Nadjemi et al. 2017) or of the PV/biomass solar hybrid energy system (Blanchet et al. 2019). Dell'Anna and Menconi (2016) investigated techno- logical solutions (such as sloping photovoltaic (PV) grids on the roofs of buildings) that can meet the energy needs of small food producers in rural areas. Off grid box is an integrated technical device developed by an Italian group, and it may have six or more 250 Wp monocrystalline crystal- line solar modules connected in parallel to a secure protec- tion control panel. A solar inverter is connected to standard AC batteries, and the same inverter can transform energy from a wind turbine (500 W), attached to the off grid box structure (Dell'Anna and Menconi 2016). On the market can be found small turbines with horizontal axis and vertical axis which can be used in rural area and they can be cho- sen based on wind conditions and type of terrain (Messineo and Culotta 2012). Renewable energy can be obtained in the form of biogas through anaerobic digestion of a variety of organic waste streams. According to Traub et al. (2021), there is a highest interest among the small dairy producers to implement anaerobic digestion system. In the last years, Traub et al. (2021) developed modular small-farm anaerobic digestion technologies for a variety of food and agricultural waste types. GWP, compared to the other two scenarios, being followed by the scenario that includes the use of energy produced from photovoltaic panels. Also, the energy production sce- narios were investigated by applying Tukey pairwise com- parisons, considering data obtained for the two environmen- tal impact categories. From Table 2, it can be observed that the means for scenarios S3 and S2 are significantly different, while S1 is not significantly different than S3 and S2.

Regarding HTP, S2 has a lower contribution to this impact category being followed by S3 and S1. In the case of acidification potential, the largest contribution to this impact category has S3, followed by S1 and S2. The same was observed for EP. The results were shown to the cheese company, and an economic and technical analysis will be made to establish the optimal option for this company.
Changes from 70 to 100% in cheese production lead to an increase of 9.25% in AP, 7.36% in EP and 7.13% in GWP, and the ADPe and HTP categories are not influenced by
Fig. 7 Sensitivity analysis variation of environmental impacts: a) ADPf and b) GWP associated with energy produc- tion scenarios
Means that do not share a letter are significantly different

these changes, the contribution is the same regardless of the allocation factor (Fig. 8a). An increase of 10% in the allocation percentage (i.e. from 70 to 80, from 80 to 90 or from 90 to 100%) resulted in increases in AP (3.08%), EP (2.45%) and GWP (2.37%). If an allocation of 20% (from 70 to 90 or 80 to 100%) is taken into account, a maximum increase of 6.17% for AP is reached. When ReCiPe method is considered, it was observed that changes from 70 to 100% in cheese production lead to an increase of 5.22% in PMF and 2.58% in TA. CC and POF impact categories were less than 1% affected by these changes, while FD and FET were not influenced at all (Fig. 8b). PMF and TA increased with 1.74% and, respectively, 0.86% when an increase of 10% in the allocation percentage was performed. A maximum increase of 3.48% is obtained when allocation of 20% is considered.
14: Characterization results obtained by applying CML 2001-January 2016 method showed that cheese technologi- cal process mainly contributes to acidification potential,

eutrophication potential and global warming potential due to the diesel used in some stages (Fig. 4). Raw milk pro- duction, sodium chloride, diesel and electricity account for 51.38%, 20.66%, 19.45% and 6.23% to the abiotic depletion potential elements. Results indicate that 52.10%, 36.37% and 7.58% of the global warming potential were due to the diesel used in technological process of cheese, raw milk production and diesel production. It was found that wastewater treat- ment contributes with the highest percentage (95.32%) to the human toxicity potential. The most important input for acidification potential is cheese production (87.55%). LCA results indicated that 53.64% and 39.65% of the eutrophi- cation potential were derived from cheese and raw milk productions.

The results obtained by applying ReCiPe 1.08 mid- point (H) method, for determination of environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese production, are illustrated in Fig. 5. The technological process of cheese produc- tion is responsible with 82.36% for the climate change impact category, followed by diesel production (11.70%),

electricity production (4.87%) and landfilling with 1.07%. Electricity mix production and burning of fossil fuels were also indicated by Nunes et al. (2020) as contribu- tors to climate change. Regarding the fossil depletion, it was observed that diesel production is the main con- tributor to this impact category with 95.46%. Wastewater treatment (43.58%), raw milk production (37.52%), diesel production (12.34%) and landfilling (6.30%) contribute to freshwater eutrophication. In terms of the particu- late matter formation impact category, it was observed that raw milk production has the largest contribution (71.04%), followed by cheese production (21.56%), die- sel production with a contribution of 4.62%, electricity with 3.02% and the others with a contribution below 1%. Diesel production (51.12%), cheese technological process (32.16%), electricity production (14.39%) and landfilling (1.07%) contributes to photochemical oxidant formation. Results indicates that cheese technological process, die- sel production and electricity production contribute with 60.09%, 22.98% and 16.53% to terrestrial acidification.

Fig. 4 CML 2001- Jan. 2016 evaluation method charac- terisation results (in %) for each impact categories (ADP e-abiotic depletion potential elements, AP - acidification potential, EP - eutrophica- tion potential, GWP - global warming potential, HTP- human toxicity potential)

Fig. 5 Environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese produc- tion obtained by using ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) method (characterisation results, in %) (CC-climate change, FD-fos- sil depletion, FET-freshwater eutrophication, PMF-par- ticulate matter formation, POF- photochemical oxidant formation, TA-terrestrial acidification)

Normalization results are presented in Fig. 6. Accord- ing to Fig. 6a, production of 1 kg sheep cheese mainly contributes to human toxicity potential. The contribu- tion of the sheep cheese production (SCP) process for the impact categories decreases in the following order: HTP > AP > GWP > EP. Results showed that distribution of sheep cheese contributes mainly to AP and GWP. It was observed that contribution of this stage to the inves- tigated impact categories decreases in the following order: AP > GWP > EP > HTP. Therefore, transportation contrib- utes more significantly to AP, GWP and EP, than to the other impact categories to which it has lower contributions. It was determined that from the total contribution of SCP and TS to AP: in the case of the first scenario, 35% contributes SCP and 65% TS1, for the second scenario the contribution of the production process decreased and contribution of transport (TS2) increased to 85%, while for the last scenario contribu- tion of transport (TS3) increased to 89%. The same situation

was observed for EP. In the case of GWP, SCP contributes with 47% and TS1 with 53%, SCP contributes with 24% and TS2 with 76% and finally SCP contributes with 18% and TS3 with 82%. As expected, increasing the distance for sheep cheese distribution leads to increased environmental impact, more specifically for AP, GWP and EP, due to the emissions from diesel combustion.

From Fig. 6b, it can be observed that SCP contributes especially to FET, followed by the contribution to CC, FD and others impact categories. Transport phase contribution to the investigated impact categories decreases in the fol- lowing order CC > PMF > TA > POF. The total contribution of SCP and TS to CC differs depending on the transport scenario: 71% SCP and 29% TS1; 46% SCP and 54% TS2; 37% SCP and 63% TS3. It was also observed that also for the other impact categories mentioned above (PMF, TA and POF) the contribution of distribution stage increases with increasing transport distances (Fig. 6b).

Fig. 6 a) CML2001- Jan. 2016 and b) ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) normalisation results (Europe reference) related to 1 kg of sheep cheese produc- tion (SCP) and transported (considering different trans- port scenarios (TS): 112 km, 266 km, 469 km), PE - person equivalents
According to different LCA studies, the electricity con-

sumption can vary significantly, depending on the type of cheese, process conditions and the plant age and size, from 0.36 kWh kg- of cheese (Berlin 2002) to 1.12 kWh kg-1 of cheese (Vagnoni et al. 2017). Ladha-Sabur et al. (2019) calculated an average of 0.30 kWh kg of cheese, while González-García et al. (2013b) established that electricity consumption can be 0.71 kWh kg-1 of cheese. The energy consumption for cheese production in this study was esti- mated at 0.83 kWh kg of cheese and falls within the val- ues found in the literature. Electricity grid mix in Romania, according to database used (GaBi 2018), included in 2014 the following energy sources: 29.36% hydro, 26.62% lignite, 17.78% nuclear, 12.34% natural gas and others. Although in 2014 it experienced an unprecedented development, the production based on unconventional sources (wind, pho- tovoltaic, biomass) had a share of 11.98% of the annual energy delivered in networks (ANRE 2015). According to Eurostat (2021), the price of electrical energy for non- household's medium size consumers varied in the last years from 0.0753 EUR/kwh in 2014 to 0.0890 EUR/kwh in 2020. Renewable energy sources are known as a major alternative to sustainable energy, by reducing greenhouse gas emis- sions and energy costs (Burloiu and Rusu 2021; Vrínceanu et al. 2022). There are a number of studies that verify the feasibility of photovoltaic systems, wind turbines in dairy farms (Bey et al. 2016; Nacer et al. 2016a, b), of the two integrated systems (PV/wind) (Nadjemi et al. 2017) or of the PV/biomass solar hybrid energy system (Blanchet et al. 2019). Dell'Anna and Menconi (2016) investigated techno- logical solutions (such as sloping photovoltaic (PV) grids on the roofs of buildings) that can meet the energy needs of small food producers in rural areas. Off grid box is an integrated technical device developed by an Italian group, and it may have six or more 250 Wp monocrystalline crystal- line solar modules connected in parallel to a secure protec- tion control panel. A solar inverter is connected to standard AC batteries, and the same inverter can transform energy from a wind turbine (500 W), attached to the off grid box structure (Dell'Anna and Menconi 2016). On the market can be found small turbines with horizontal axis and vertical axis which can be used in rural area and they can be cho- sen based on wind conditions and type of terrain (Messineo and Culotta 2012). Renewable energy can be obtained in the form of biogas through anaerobic digestion of a variety of organic waste streams. According to Traub et al. (2021), there is a highest interest among the small dairy producers to implement anaerobic digestion system. In the last years, Traub et al. (2021) developed modular small-farm anaerobic digestion technologies for a variety of food and agricultural waste types. The dairy site investigated in this study is situ- ated in a geographical area with an average annual wind speed of 3.99 m/s at 10 m height (NEWA 2022) and average|according to Paraschiv and Paraschiv (2018) the duration of sunlight ranges between 2.2 (in January) and 10.5 (in July) hours per day. Therefore, the main ecological and economic improve- ment of the dairy factory can be addressed to the use of energy from renewable sources. The processing capacity is currently reduced, but it plans to expand it and also takes into account the installation of solar panels for electricity production and of solar boilers. Considering this aspect, in order to provide data on the impact on the environment resulting from the production of different types of energy, three scenarios were developed: S1 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity production from solar photovoltaic, S2 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity production from wind, while S3 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity produc- tion from biogas. The GaBi databases referring to Romanian electrical energy were used: RO: power from biogas-alter- nating current (AC), mix of direct and combined heat and power plants (CHP), technology mix regarding firing and flue gas cleaning, 1 kV-60 kV; RO: power from photovolta- ics-AC, technology mix of CIS, CdTE, monocrystalline and multi-crystalline, 1 kV-601 RO: power from wind power- AC, technology mix of onshore and offshore, 1 kV-60 kV. The environmental impact of renewable energy production from these three different sources was determined. It was considered its use in different percentages 5, 10 and 20% in the process of obtaining cheese. In the sensitivity analysis, the following composition of energy sources were consid- ered for S1 95% grid mix and 5% solar energy, 90% grid mix and 10% solar energy, 80% grid mix and 20% solar energy; for 95% grid mix and 5% wind energy, 90% grid mix and 10% wind energy, 80% grid mix and 20% wind energy; and for S3 95% grid mix and 5% biogas energy, 90% grid mix and 10% biogas energy, 80% grid mix and 20% biogas energy. The panels selected for this study were considered to have a maximum power of 120 W (but there are also panels in 200-350 W) and can be installed at an angle of 30° to the horizontal. It has been assumed that the PV panels are used around 4-h of sun per day and 0.48 kWh per panel is obtained. There are needed 62 panels, and the maximum power of the photovoltaic system is around 7 kW; however, the system is producing more in summer months and less in winter. The number of panels can increase or decrease depending on the amount of energy we want to get. Most probably, the wind turbine will operate for 4 h/day from April to September and for 7 h/day between October and March, approximately 2007 h/year. It was assumed that for a wind turbine (12 m diameter and average wind speed m/s, under standard atmospheric conditions 1.225 kg/ m³) it can be obtained an annual energy production of 3556 kWh. In real conditions, the variability of the wind regime
solar radiation of 3.55 kWh/m2/day (EC 2022). In Romania,

and the characteristics of wind turbine will greatly influ- ence the output. The calculations were performed based on equations provided by Paraschiv and Paraschiv (2018). It was assumed that the biogas can be obtained from sheep manure and other biomass at dairy farm. There is potential for biogas production from manure; for example, Vac et al. (2013) investigated the greenhouse gas emissions from a farm with a capacity of 600 sheep, manure surface of 579 m², the measured emissions for sheep manure were: 0.83 t CH4 /year and 61.3 t CO2 /year. These emissions may not reach the atmosphere and be harnessed through anaerobic digestion. The manure production can be between 0.3 and 0.4 kg/sheep/day, depending on the animal breed and age. Future studies (technical and economic) will investigate these ways of obtaining electricity in more detail.

Diesel production (51.12%), cheese technological process (32.16%), electricity production (14.39%) and landfilling (1.07%) contributes to photochemical oxidant formation. Results indicates that cheese technological process, die- sel production and electricity production contribute with 60.09%, 22.98% and 16.53% to terrestrial acidification.

Fig. 4 CML 2001- Jan. 2016 evaluation method charac- terisation results (in %) for each impact categories (ADP e-abiotic depletion potential elements, AP - acidification potential, EP - eutrophica- tion potential, GWP - global warming potential, HTP- human toxicity potential)

Fig. 5 Environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese produc- tion obtained by using ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) method (characterisation results, in %) (CC-climate change, FD-fos- sil depletion, FET-freshwater eutrophication, PMF-par- ticulate matter formation, POF- photochemical oxidant formation, TA-terrestrial acidification)

Normalization results are presented in Fig. 6. Accord- ing to Fig. 6a, production of 1 kg sheep cheese mainly contributes to human toxicity potential. The contribu- tion of the sheep cheese production (SCP) process for the impact categories decreases in the following order: HTP > AP > GWP > EP. Results showed that distribution of sheep cheese contributes mainly to AP and GWP. It was observed that contribution of this stage to the inves- tigated impact categories decreases in the following order: AP > GWP > EP > HTP. Therefore, transportation contrib- utes more significantly to AP, GWP and EP, than to the other impact categories to which it has lower contributions. It was determined that from the total contribution of SCP and TS to AP: in the case of the first scenario, 35% contributes SCP and 65% TS1, for the second scenario the contribution of the production process decreased and contribution of transport (TS2) increased to 85%, while for the last scenario contribu- tion of transport (TS3) increased to 89%. The same situation

was observed for EP. In the case of GWP, SCP contributes with 47% and TS1 with 53%, SCP contributes with 24% and TS2 with 76% and finally SCP contributes with 18% and TS3 with 82%. As expected, increasing the distance for sheep cheese distribution leads to increased environmental impact, more specifically for AP, GWP and EP, due to the emissions from diesel combustion.

From Fig. 6b, it can be observed that SCP contributes especially to FET, followed by the contribution to CC, FD and others impact categories. Transport phase contribution to the investigated impact categories decreases in the fol- lowing order CC > PMF > TA > POF. The total contribution of SCP and TS to CC differs depending on the transport scenario: 71% SCP and 29% TS1; 46% SCP and 54% TS2; 37% SCP and 63% TS3. It was also observed that also for the other impact categories mentioned above (PMF, TA and POF) the contribution of distribution stage increases with increasing transport distances (Fig. 6b).

Fig. 6 a) CML2001- Jan. 2016 and b) ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) normalisation results (Europe reference) related to 1 kg of sheep cheese produc- tion (SCP) and transported (considering different trans- port scenarios (TS): 112 km, 266 km, 469 km), PE - person equivalents
According to different LCA studies, the electricity con-

sumption can vary significantly, depending on the type of cheese, process conditions and the plant age and size, from 0.36 kWh kg- of cheese (Berlin 2002) to 1.12 kWh kg-1 of cheese (Vagnoni et al. 2017). Ladha-Sabur et al. (2019) calculated an average of 0.30 kWh kg of cheese, while González-García et al. (2013b) established that electricity consumption can be 0.71 kWh kg-1 of cheese. The energy consumption for cheese production in this study was esti- mated at 0.83 kWh kg of cheese and falls within the val- ues found in the literature. Electricity grid mix in Romania, according to database used (GaBi 2018), included in 2014 the following energy sources: 29.36% hydro, 26.62% lignite, 17.78% nuclear, 12.34% natural gas and others. Although in 2014 it experienced an unprecedented development, the production based on unconventional sources (wind, pho- tovoltaic, biomass) had a share of 11.98% of the annual energy delivered in networks (ANRE 2015). According to Eurostat (2021), the price of electrical energy for non- household's medium size consumers varied in the last years from 0.0753 EUR/kwh in 2014 to 0.0890 EUR/kwh in 2020. Renewable energy sources are known as a major alternative to sustainable energy, by reducing greenhouse gas emis- sions and energy costs (Burloiu and Rusu 2021; Vrínceanu et al. 2022). There are a number of studies that verify the feasibility of photovoltaic systems, wind turbines in dairy farms (Bey et al. 2016; Nacer et al. 2016a, b), of the two integrated systems (PV/wind) (Nadjemi et al. 2017) or of the PV/biomass solar hybrid energy system (Blanchet et al. 2019). Dell'Anna and Menconi (2016) investigated techno- logical solutions (such as sloping photovoltaic (PV) grids on the roofs of buildings) that can meet the energy needs of small food producers in rural areas. Off grid box is an integrated technical device developed by an Italian group, and it may have six or more 250 Wp monocrystalline crystal- line solar modules connected in parallel to a secure protec- tion control panel. A solar inverter is connected to standard AC batteries, and the same inverter can transform energy from a wind turbine (500 W), attached to the off grid box structure (Dell'Anna and Menconi 2016). On the market can be found small turbines with horizontal axis and vertical axis which can be used in rural area and they can be cho- sen based on wind conditions and type of terrain (Messineo and Culotta 2012). Renewable energy can be obtained in the form of biogas through anaerobic digestion of a variety of organic waste streams. According to Traub et al. (2021), there is a highest interest among the small dairy producers to implement anaerobic digestion system. In the last years, Traub et al. (2021) developed modular small-farm anaerobic digestion technologies for a variety of food and agricultural waste types. The dairy site investigated in this study is situ- ated in a geographical area with an average annual wind speed of 3.99 m/s at 10 m height (NEWA 2022) and average|according to Paraschiv and Paraschiv (2018) the duration of sunlight ranges between 2.2 (in January) and 10.5 (in July) hours per day. Therefore, the main ecological and economic improve- ment of the dairy factory can be addressed to the use of energy from renewable sources. The processing capacity is currently reduced, but it plans to expand it and also takes into account the installation of solar panels for electricity production and of solar boilers. Considering this aspect, in order to provide data on the impact on the environment resulting from the production of different types of energy, three scenarios were developed: S1 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity production from solar photovoltaic, S2 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity production from wind, while S3 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity produc- tion from biogas. The GaBi databases referring to Romanian electrical energy were used: RO: power from biogas-alter- nating current (AC), mix of direct and combined heat and power plants (CHP), technology mix regarding firing and flue gas cleaning, 1 kV-60 kV; RO: power from photovolta- ics-AC, technology mix of CIS, CdTE, monocrystalline and multi-crystalline, 1 kV-601 RO: power from wind power- AC, technology mix of onshore and offshore, 1 kV-60 kV. The environmental impact of renewable energy production from these three different sources was determined. It was considered its use in different percentages 5, 10 and 20% in the process of obtaining cheese. In the sensitivity analysis, the following composition of energy sources were consid- ered for S1 95% grid mix and 5% solar energy, 90% grid mix and 10% solar energy, 80% grid mix and 20% solar energy; for 95% grid mix and 5% wind energy, 90% grid mix and 10% wind energy, 80% grid mix and 20% wind energy; and for S3 95% grid mix and 5% biogas energy, 90% grid mix and 10% biogas energy, 80% grid mix and 20% biogas energy. The panels selected for this study were considered to have a maximum power of 120 W (but there are also panels in 200-350 W) and can be installed at an angle of 30° to the horizontal. It has been assumed that the PV panels are used around 4-h of sun per day and 0.48 kWh per panel is obtained. There are needed 62 panels, and the maximum power of the photovoltaic system is around 7 kW; however, the system is producing more in summer months and less in winter. The number of panels can increase or decrease depending on the amount of energy we want to get. Most probably, the wind turbine will operate for 4 h/day from April to September and for 7 h/day between October and March, approximately 2007 h/year. It was assumed that for a wind turbine (12 m diameter and average wind speed m/s, under standard atmospheric conditions 1.225 kg/ m³) it can be obtained an annual energy production of 3556 kWh. In real conditions, the variability of the wind regime
solar radiation of 3.55 kWh/m2/day (EC 2022). In Romania,

and the characteristics of wind turbine will greatly influ- ence the output. The calculations were performed based on equations provided by Paraschiv and Paraschiv (2018). It was assumed that the biogas can be obtained from sheep manure and other biomass at dairy farm. There is potential for biogas production from manure; for example, Vac et al. (2013) investigated the greenhouse gas emissions from a farm with a capacity of 600 sheep, manure surface of 579 m², the measured emissions for sheep manure were: 0.83 t CH4 /year and 61.3 t CO2 /year. These emissions may not reach the atmosphere and be harnessed through anaerobic digestion. The manure production can be between 0.3 and 0.4 kg/sheep/day, depending on the animal breed and age. Future studies (technical and economic) will investigate these ways of obtaining electricity in more detail.
From the sensitivity analysis (Fig. 7), it can be seen that the scenario which includes the energy produced by the wind has smaller impacts on the environment, regarding ADPf,

GWP, compared to the other two scenarios, being followed by the scenario that includes the use of energy produced from photovoltaic panels. Also, the energy production sce- narios were investigated by applying Tukey pairwise com- parisons, considering data obtained for the two environmen- tal impact categories. From Table 2, it can be observed that the means for scenarios S3 and S2 are significantly different, while S1 is not significantly different than S3 and S2.

Regarding HTP, S2 has a lower contribution to this impact category being followed by S3 and S1. In the case of acidification potential, the largest contribution to this impact category has S3, followed by S1 and S2. The same was observed for EP. The results were shown to the cheese company, and an economic and technical analysis will be made to establish the optimal option for this company.
Changes from 70 to 100% in cheese production lead to an increase of 9.25% in AP, 7.36% in EP and 7.13% in GWP, and the ADPe and HTP categories are not influenced by
Fig. 7 Sensitivity analysis variation of environmental impacts: a) ADPf and b) GWP associated with energy produc- tion scenarios
Means that do not share a letter are significantly different

these changes, the contribution is the same regardless of the allocation factor (Fig. 8a). An increase of 10% in the allocation percentage (i.e. from 70 to 80, from 80 to 90 or from 90 to 100%) resulted in increases in AP (3.08%), EP (2.45%) and GWP (2.37%). If an allocation of 20% (from 70 to 90 or 80 to 100%) is taken into account, a maximum increase of 6.17% for AP is reached. When ReCiPe method is considered, it was observed that changes from 70 to 100% in cheese production lead to an increase of 5.22% in PMF and 2.58% in TA. CC and POF impact categories were less than 1% affected by these changes, while FD and FET were not influenced at all (Fig. 8b). PMF and TA increased with 1.74% and, respectively, 0.86% when an increase of 10% in the allocation percentage was performed. A maximum increase of 3.48% is obtained when allocation of 20% is considered.
17: Characterization results obtained by applying CML 2001-January 2016 method showed that cheese technologi- cal process mainly contributes to acidification potential,

eutrophication potential and global warming potential due to the diesel used in some stages (Fig. 4). Raw milk pro- duction, sodium chloride, diesel and electricity account for 51.38%, 20.66%, 19.45% and 6.23% to the abiotic depletion potential elements. Results indicate that 52.10%, 36.37% and 7.58% of the global warming potential were due to the diesel used in technological process of cheese, raw milk production and diesel production. It was found that wastewater treat- ment contributes with the highest percentage (95.32%) to the human toxicity potential. Results indicates that cheese technological process, die- sel production and electricity production contribute with 60.09%, 22.98% and 16.53% to terrestrial acidification.

The energy consumption for cheese production in this study was esti- mated at 0.83 kWh kg of cheese and falls within the val- ues found in the literature. Electricity grid mix in Romania, according to database used (GaBi 2018), included in 2014 the following energy sources: 29.36% hydro, 26.62% lignite, 17.78% nuclear, 12.34% natural gas and others. Although in 2014 it experienced an unprecedented development, the production based on unconventional sources (wind, pho- tovoltaic, biomass) had a share of 11.98% of the annual energy delivered in networks (ANRE 2015). According to Eurostat (2021), the price of electrical energy for non- household's medium size consumers varied in the last years from 0.0753 EUR/kwh in 2014 to 0.0890 EUR/kwh in 2020. Renewable energy sources are known as a major alternative to sustainable energy, by reducing greenhouse gas emis- sions and energy costs (Burloiu and Rusu 2021; Vrínceanu et al. 2022). There are a number of studies that verify the feasibility of photovoltaic systems, wind turbines in dairy farms (Bey et al. 2016; Nacer et al. 2016a, b), of the two integrated systems (PV/wind) (Nadjemi et al. 2017) or of the PV/biomass solar hybrid energy system (Blanchet et al. 2019). Dell'Anna and Menconi (2016) investigated techno- logical solutions (such as sloping photovoltaic (PV) grids on the roofs of buildings) that can meet the energy needs of small food producers in rural areas. Off grid box is an integrated technical device developed by an Italian group, and it may have six or more 250 Wp monocrystalline crystal- line solar modules connected in parallel to a secure protec- tion control panel. A solar inverter is connected to standard AC batteries, and the same inverter can transform energy from a wind turbine (500 W), attached to the off grid box structure (Dell'Anna and Menconi 2016). On the market can be found small turbines with horizontal axis and vertical axis which can be used in rural area and they can be cho- sen based on wind conditions and type of terrain (Messineo and Culotta 2012). Renewable energy can be obtained in the form of biogas through anaerobic digestion of a variety of organic waste streams. According to Traub et al. (2021), there is a highest interest among the small dairy producers to implement anaerobic digestion system. In the last years, Traub et al. (2021) developed modular small-farm anaerobic digestion technologies for a variety of food and agricultural waste types. The dairy site investigated in this study is situ- ated in a geographical area with an average annual wind speed of 3.99 m/s at 10 m height (NEWA 2022) and average|according to Paraschiv and Paraschiv (2018) the duration of sunlight ranges between 2.2 (in January) and 10.5 (in July) hours per day. Therefore, the main ecological and economic improve- ment of the dairy factory can be addressed to the use of energy from renewable sources. The processing capacity is currently reduced, but it plans to expand it and also takes into account the installation of solar panels for electricity production and of solar boilers. Considering this aspect, in order to provide data on the impact on the environment resulting from the production of different types of energy, three scenarios were developed: S1 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity production from solar photovoltaic, S2 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity production from wind, while S3 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity produc- tion from biogas. The GaBi databases referring to Romanian electrical energy were used: RO: power from biogas-alter- nating current (AC), mix of direct and combined heat and power plants (CHP), technology mix regarding firing and flue gas cleaning, 1 kV-60 kV; RO: power from photovolta- ics-AC, technology mix of CIS, CdTE, monocrystalline and multi-crystalline, 1 kV-601 RO: power from wind power- AC, technology mix of onshore and offshore, 1 kV-60 kV. The environmental impact of renewable energy production from these three different sources was determined. It was considered its use in different percentages 5, 10 and 20% in the process of obtaining cheese. In the sensitivity analysis, the following composition of energy sources were consid- ered for S1 95% grid mix and 5% solar energy, 90% grid mix and 10% solar energy, 80% grid mix and 20% solar energy; for 95% grid mix and 5% wind energy, 90% grid mix and 10% wind energy, 80% grid mix and 20% wind energy; and for S3 95% grid mix and 5% biogas energy, 90% grid mix and 10% biogas energy, 80% grid mix and 20% biogas energy. The panels selected for this study were considered to have a maximum power of 120 W (but there are also panels in 200-350 W) and can be installed at an angle of 30° to the horizontal. It has been assumed that the PV panels are used around 4-h of sun per day and 0.48 kWh per panel is obtained. There are needed 62 panels, and the maximum power of the photovoltaic system is around 7 kW; however, the system is producing more in summer months and less in winter. The number of panels can increase or decrease depending on the amount of energy we want to get. Most probably, the wind turbine will operate for 4 h/day from April to September and for 7 h/day between October and March, approximately 2007 h/year. It was assumed that for a wind turbine (12 m diameter and average wind speed m/s, under standard atmospheric conditions 1.225 kg/ m³) it can be obtained an annual energy production of 3556 kWh. In real conditions, the variability of the wind regime
solar radiation of 3.55 kWh/m2/day (EC 2022). In Romania,

and the characteristics of wind turbine will greatly influ- ence the output. The calculations were performed based on equations provided by Paraschiv and Paraschiv (2018). It was assumed that the biogas can be obtained from sheep manure and other biomass at dairy farm. There is potential for biogas production from manure; for example, Vac et al. (2013) investigated the greenhouse gas emissions from a farm with a capacity of 600 sheep, manure surface of 579 m², the measured emissions for sheep manure were: 0.83 t CH4 /year and 61.3 t CO2 /year. These emissions may not reach the atmosphere and be harnessed through anaerobic digestion. The manure production can be between 0.3 and 0.4 kg/sheep/day, depending on the animal breed and age. Future studies (technical and economic) will investigate these ways of obtaining electricity in more detail.
From the sensitivity analysis (Fig. 7), it can be seen that the scenario which includes the energy produced by the wind has smaller impacts on the environment, regarding ADPf,

GWP, compared to the other two scenarios, being followed by the scenario that includes the use of energy produced from photovoltaic panels. Also, the energy production sce- narios were investigated by applying Tukey pairwise com- parisons, considering data obtained for the two environmen- tal impact categories. From Table 2, it can be observed that the means for scenarios S3 and S2 are significantly different, while S1 is not significantly different than S3 and S2.

Regarding HTP, S2 has a lower contribution to this impact category being followed by S3 and S1. In the case of acidification potential, the largest contribution to this impact category has S3, followed by S1 and S2. The same was observed for EP. The results were shown to the cheese company, and an economic and technical analysis will be made to establish the optimal option for this company.
Changes from 70 to 100% in cheese production lead to an increase of 9.25% in AP, 7.36% in EP and 7.13% in GWP, and the ADPe and HTP categories are not influenced by
Fig. 7 Sensitivity analysis variation of environmental impacts: a) ADPf and b) GWP associated with energy produc- tion scenarios
Means that do not share a letter are significantly different

these changes, the contribution is the same regardless of the allocation factor (Fig. 8a). An increase of 10% in the allocation percentage (i.e. from 70 to 80, from 80 to 90 or from 90 to 100%) resulted in increases in AP (3.08%), EP (2.45%) and GWP (2.37%). If an allocation of 20% (from 70 to 90 or 80 to 100%) is taken into account, a maximum increase of 6.17% for AP is reached. When ReCiPe method is considered, it was observed that changes from 70 to 100% in cheese production lead to an increase of 5.22% in PMF and 2.58% in TA. CC and POF impact categories were less than 1% affected by these changes, while FD and FET were not influenced at all (Fig. 8b). PMF and TA increased with 1.74% and, respectively, 0.86% when an increase of 10% in the allocation percentage was performed. A maximum increase of 3.48% is obtained when allocation of 20% is considered.
19: Characterization results obtained by applying CML 2001-January 2016 method showed that cheese technologi- cal process mainly contributes to acidification potential,

eutrophication potential and global warming potential due to the diesel used in some stages (Fig. 4). Raw milk pro- duction, sodium chloride, diesel and electricity account for 51.38%, 20.66%, 19.45% and 6.23% to the abiotic depletion potential elements. Results indicate that 52.10%, 36.37% and 7.58% of the global warming potential were due to the diesel used in technological process of cheese, raw milk production and diesel production. It was found that wastewater treat- ment contributes with the highest percentage (95.32%) to the human toxicity potential. The most important input for acidification potential is cheese production (87.55%). LCA results indicated that 53.64% and 39.65% of the eutrophi- cation potential were derived from cheese and raw milk productions.

The results obtained by applying ReCiPe 1.08 mid- point (H) method, for determination of environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese production, are illustrated in Fig. 5. The technological process of cheese produc- tion is responsible with 82.36% for the climate change impact category, followed by diesel production (11.70%),

electricity production (4.87%) and landfilling with 1.07%. Electricity mix production and burning of fossil fuels were also indicated by Nunes et al. (2020) as contribu- tors to climate change. Regarding the fossil depletion, it was observed that diesel production is the main con- tributor to this impact category with 95.46%. Wastewater treatment (43.58%), raw milk production (37.52%), diesel production (12.34%) and landfilling (6.30%) contribute to freshwater eutrophication. In terms of the particu- late matter formation impact category, it was observed that raw milk production has the largest contribution (71.04%), followed by cheese production (21.56%), die- sel production with a contribution of 4.62%, electricity with 3.02% and the others with a contribution below 1%. Diesel production (51.12%), cheese technological process (32.16%), electricity production (14.39%) and landfilling (1.07%) contributes to photochemical oxidant formation. Results indicates that cheese technological process, die- sel production and electricity production contribute with 60.09%, 22.98% and 16.53% to terrestrial acidification.

Fig. 4 CML 2001- Jan. 2016 evaluation method charac- terisation results (in %) for each impact categories (ADP e-abiotic depletion potential elements, AP - acidification potential, EP - eutrophica- tion potential, GWP - global warming potential, HTP- human toxicity potential)

Fig. 5 Environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese produc- tion obtained by using ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) method (characterisation results, in %) (CC-climate change, FD-fos- sil depletion, FET-freshwater eutrophication, PMF-par- ticulate matter formation, POF- photochemical oxidant formation, TA-terrestrial acidification)

Normalization results are presented in Fig. 6. Accord- ing to Fig. 6a, production of 1 kg sheep cheese mainly contributes to human toxicity potential. The contribu- tion of the sheep cheese production (SCP) process for the impact categories decreases in the following order: HTP > AP > GWP > EP. Results showed that distribution of sheep cheese contributes mainly to AP and GWP. It was observed that contribution of this stage to the inves- tigated impact categories decreases in the following order: AP > GWP > EP > HTP. Therefore, transportation contrib- utes more significantly to AP, GWP and EP, than to the other impact categories to which it has lower contributions. It was determined that from the total contribution of SCP and TS to AP: in the case of the first scenario, 35% contributes SCP and 65% TS1, for the second scenario the contribution of the production process decreased and contribution of transport (TS2) increased to 85%, while for the last scenario contribu- tion of transport (TS3) increased to 89%. The same situation

was observed for EP. In the case of GWP, SCP contributes with 47% and TS1 with 53%, SCP contributes with 24% and TS2 with 76% and finally SCP contributes with 18% and TS3 with 82%. As expected, increasing the distance for sheep cheese distribution leads to increased environmental impact, more specifically for AP, GWP and EP, due to the emissions from diesel combustion.

From Fig. 6b, it can be observed that SCP contributes especially to FET, followed by the contribution to CC, FD and others impact categories. Transport phase contribution to the investigated impact categories decreases in the fol- lowing order CC > PMF > TA > POF. The total contribution of SCP and TS to CC differs depending on the transport scenario: 71% SCP and 29% TS1; 46% SCP and 54% TS2; 37% SCP and 63% TS3. It was also observed that also for the other impact categories mentioned above (PMF, TA and POF) the contribution of distribution stage increases with increasing transport distances (Fig. 6b).

The GaBi databases referring to Romanian electrical energy were used: RO: power from biogas-alter- nating current (AC), mix of direct and combined heat and power plants (CHP), technology mix regarding firing and flue gas cleaning, 1 kV-60 kV; RO: power from photovolta- ics-AC, technology mix of CIS, CdTE, monocrystalline and multi-crystalline, 1 kV-601 RO: power from wind power- AC, technology mix of onshore and offshore, 1 kV-60 kV. The environmental impact of renewable energy production from these three different sources was determined. It was considered its use in different percentages 5, 10 and 20% in the process of obtaining cheese. In the sensitivity analysis, the following composition of energy sources were consid- ered for S1 95% grid mix and 5% solar energy, 90% grid mix and 10% solar energy, 80% grid mix and 20% solar energy; for 95% grid mix and 5% wind energy, 90% grid mix and 10% wind energy, 80% grid mix and 20% wind energy; and for S3 95% grid mix and 5% biogas energy, 90% grid mix and 10% biogas energy, 80% grid mix and 20% biogas energy. The panels selected for this study were considered to have a maximum power of 120 W (but there are also panels in 200-350 W) and can be installed at an angle of 30° to the horizontal. It has been assumed that the PV panels are used around 4-h of sun per day and 0.48 kWh per panel is obtained. There are needed 62 panels, and the maximum power of the photovoltaic system is around 7 kW; however, the system is producing more in summer months and less in winter. The number of panels can increase or decrease depending on the amount of energy we want to get. Most probably, the wind turbine will operate for 4 h/day from April to September and for 7 h/day between October and March, approximately 2007 h/year. It was assumed that for a wind turbine (12 m diameter and average wind speed m/s, under standard atmospheric conditions 1.225 kg/ m³) it can be obtained an annual energy production of 3556 kWh. In real conditions, the variability of the wind regime
solar radiation of 3.55 kWh/m2/day (EC 2022). In Romania,

and the characteristics of wind turbine will greatly influ- ence the output. The calculations were performed based on equations provided by Paraschiv and Paraschiv (2018). It was assumed that the biogas can be obtained from sheep manure and other biomass at dairy farm. There is potential for biogas production from manure; for example, Vac et al. (2013) investigated the greenhouse gas emissions from a farm with a capacity of 600 sheep, manure surface of 579 m², the measured emissions for sheep manure were: 0.83 t CH4 /year and 61.3 t CO2 /year. These emissions may not reach the atmosphere and be harnessed through anaerobic digestion. The manure production can be between 0.3 and 0.4 kg/sheep/day, depending on the animal breed and age. Future studies (technical and economic) will investigate these ways of obtaining electricity in more detail.
From the sensitivity analysis (Fig. 7), it can be seen that the scenario which includes the energy produced by the wind has smaller impacts on the environment, regarding ADPf,

GWP, compared to the other two scenarios, being followed by the scenario that includes the use of energy produced from photovoltaic panels. Also, the energy production sce- narios were investigated by applying Tukey pairwise com- parisons, considering data obtained for the two environmen- tal impact categories. From Table 2, it can be observed that the means for scenarios S3 and S2 are significantly different, while S1 is not significantly different than S3 and S2.

Regarding HTP, S2 has a lower contribution to this impact category being followed by S3 and S1. In the case of acidification potential, the largest contribution to this impact category has S3, followed by S1 and S2. The same was observed for EP. The results were shown to the cheese company, and an economic and technical analysis will be made to establish the optimal option for this company.

these changes, the contribution is the same regardless of the allocation factor (Fig. 8a). An increase of 10% in the allocation percentage (i.e. from 70 to 80, from 80 to 90 or from 90 to 100%) resulted in increases in AP (3.08%), EP (2.45%) and GWP (2.37%). If an allocation of 20% (from 70 to 90 or 80 to 100%) is taken into account, a maximum increase of 6.17% for AP is reached. When ReCiPe method is considered, it was observed that changes from 70 to 100% in cheese production lead to an increase of 5.22% in PMF and 2.58% in TA. CC and POF impact categories were less than 1% affected by these changes, while FD and FET were not influenced at all (Fig. 8b). PMF and TA increased with 1.74% and, respectively, 0.86% when an increase of 10% in the allocation percentage was performed. A maximum increase of 3.48% is obtained when allocation of 20% is considered.
21: Characterization results obtained by applying CML 2001-January 2016 method showed that cheese technologi- cal process mainly contributes to acidification potential,

eutrophication potential and global warming potential due to the diesel used in some stages (Fig. 4). Raw milk pro- duction, sodium chloride, diesel and electricity account for 51.38%, 20.66%, 19.45% and 6.23% to the abiotic depletion potential elements. Results indicate that 52.10%, 36.37% and 7.58% of the global warming potential were due to the diesel used in technological process of cheese, raw milk production and diesel production. It was found that wastewater treat- ment contributes with the highest percentage (95.32%) to the human toxicity potential. The most important input for acidification potential is cheese production (87.55%). LCA results indicated that 53.64% and 39.65% of the eutrophi- cation potential were derived from cheese and raw milk productions.

The results obtained by applying ReCiPe 1.08 mid- point (H) method, for determination of environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese production, are illustrated in Fig. 5. The technological process of cheese produc- tion is responsible with 82.36% for the climate change impact category, followed by diesel production (11.70%),

electricity production (4.87%) and landfilling with 1.07%. Electricity mix production and burning of fossil fuels were also indicated by Nunes et al. (2020) as contribu- tors to climate change. Regarding the fossil depletion, it was observed that diesel production is the main con- tributor to this impact category with 95.46%. Wastewater treatment (43.58%), raw milk production (37.52%), diesel production (12.34%) and landfilling (6.30%) contribute to freshwater eutrophication. In terms of the particu- late matter formation impact category, it was observed that raw milk production has the largest contribution (71.04%), followed by cheese production (21.56%), die- sel production with a contribution of 4.62%, electricity with 3.02% and the others with a contribution below 1%. Diesel production (51.12%), cheese technological process (32.16%), electricity production (14.39%) and landfilling (1.07%) contributes to photochemical oxidant formation. Results indicates that cheese technological process, die- sel production and electricity production contribute with 60.09%, 22.98% and 16.53% to terrestrial acidification.

Fig. 4 CML 2001- Jan. 2016 evaluation method charac- terisation results (in %) for each impact categories (ADP e-abiotic depletion potential elements, AP - acidification potential, EP - eutrophica- tion potential, GWP - global warming potential, HTP- human toxicity potential)

Fig. 5 Environmental impacts of 1 kg sheep cheese produc- tion obtained by using ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) method (characterisation results, in %) (CC-climate change, FD-fos- sil depletion, FET-freshwater eutrophication, PMF-par- ticulate matter formation, POF- photochemical oxidant formation, TA-terrestrial acidification)

Normalization results are presented in Fig. 6. Accord- ing to Fig. 6a, production of 1 kg sheep cheese mainly contributes to human toxicity potential. The contribu- tion of the sheep cheese production (SCP) process for the impact categories decreases in the following order: HTP > AP > GWP > EP. Results showed that distribution of sheep cheese contributes mainly to AP and GWP. It was observed that contribution of this stage to the inves- tigated impact categories decreases in the following order: AP > GWP > EP > HTP. Therefore, transportation contrib- utes more significantly to AP, GWP and EP, than to the other impact categories to which it has lower contributions. It was determined that from the total contribution of SCP and TS to AP: in the case of the first scenario, 35% contributes SCP and 65% TS1, for the second scenario the contribution of the production process decreased and contribution of transport (TS2) increased to 85%, while for the last scenario contribu- tion of transport (TS3) increased to 89%. The same situation

was observed for EP. In the case of GWP, SCP contributes with 47% and TS1 with 53%, SCP contributes with 24% and TS2 with 76% and finally SCP contributes with 18% and TS3 with 82%. As expected, increasing the distance for sheep cheese distribution leads to increased environmental impact, more specifically for AP, GWP and EP, due to the emissions from diesel combustion.

From Fig. 6b, it can be observed that SCP contributes especially to FET, followed by the contribution to CC, FD and others impact categories. Transport phase contribution to the investigated impact categories decreases in the fol- lowing order CC > PMF > TA > POF. The total contribution of SCP and TS to CC differs depending on the transport scenario: 71% SCP and 29% TS1; 46% SCP and 54% TS2; 37% SCP and 63% TS3. It was also observed that also for the other impact categories mentioned above (PMF, TA and POF) the contribution of distribution stage increases with increasing transport distances (Fig. 6b).

Fig. 6 a) CML2001- Jan. 2016 and b) ReCiPe 1.08 Midpoint (H) normalisation results (Europe reference) related to 1 kg of sheep cheese produc- tion (SCP) and transported (considering different trans- port scenarios (TS): 112 km, 266 km, 469 km), PE - person equivalents
According to different LCA studies, the electricity con-

sumption can vary significantly, depending on the type of cheese, process conditions and the plant age and size, from 0.36 kWh kg- of cheese (Berlin 2002) to 1.12 kWh kg-1 of cheese (Vagnoni et al. 2017). Ladha-Sabur et al. (2019) calculated an average of 0.30 kWh kg of cheese, while González-García et al. (2013b) established that electricity consumption can be 0.71 kWh kg-1 of cheese. The energy consumption for cheese production in this study was esti- mated at 0.83 kWh kg of cheese and falls within the val- ues found in the literature. Electricity grid mix in Romania, according to database used (GaBi 2018), included in 2014 the following energy sources: 29.36% hydro, 26.62% lignite, 17.78% nuclear, 12.34% natural gas and others. Although in 2014 it experienced an unprecedented development, the production based on unconventional sources (wind, pho- tovoltaic, biomass) had a share of 11.98% of the annual energy delivered in networks (ANRE 2015). According to Eurostat (2021), the price of electrical energy for non- household's medium size consumers varied in the last years from 0.0753 EUR/kwh in 2014 to 0.0890 EUR/kwh in 2020. Renewable energy sources are known as a major alternative to sustainable energy, by reducing greenhouse gas emis- sions and energy costs (Burloiu and Rusu 2021; Vrínceanu et al. 2022). There are a number of studies that verify the feasibility of photovoltaic systems, wind turbines in dairy farms (Bey et al. 2016; Nacer et al. 2016a, b), of the two integrated systems (PV/wind) (Nadjemi et al. 2017) or of the PV/biomass solar hybrid energy system (Blanchet et al. 2019). Dell'Anna and Menconi (2016) investigated techno- logical solutions (such as sloping photovoltaic (PV) grids on the roofs of buildings) that can meet the energy needs of small food producers in rural areas. Off grid box is an integrated technical device developed by an Italian group, and it may have six or more 250 Wp monocrystalline crystal- line solar modules connected in parallel to a secure protec- tion control panel. A solar inverter is connected to standard AC batteries, and the same inverter can transform energy from a wind turbine (500 W), attached to the off grid box structure (Dell'Anna and Menconi 2016). On the market can be found small turbines with horizontal axis and vertical axis which can be used in rural area and they can be cho- sen based on wind conditions and type of terrain (Messineo and Culotta 2012). Renewable energy can be obtained in the form of biogas through anaerobic digestion of a variety of organic waste streams. According to Traub et al. (2021), there is a highest interest among the small dairy producers to implement anaerobic digestion system. In the last years, Traub et al. (2021) developed modular small-farm anaerobic digestion technologies for a variety of food and agricultural waste types. The dairy site investigated in this study is situ- ated in a geographical area with an average annual wind speed of 3.99 m/s at 10 m height (NEWA 2022) and average|according to Paraschiv and Paraschiv (2018) the duration of sunlight ranges between 2.2 (in January) and 10.5 (in July) hours per day. Therefore, the main ecological and economic improve- ment of the dairy factory can be addressed to the use of energy from renewable sources. The processing capacity is currently reduced, but it plans to expand it and also takes into account the installation of solar panels for electricity production and of solar boilers. Considering this aspect, in order to provide data on the impact on the environment resulting from the production of different types of energy, three scenarios were developed: S1 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity production from solar photovoltaic, S2 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity production from wind, while S3 includes electricity obtained from grid mix and electricity produc- tion from biogas. The environmental impact of renewable energy production from these three different sources was determined. It was considered its use in different percentages 5, 10 and 20% in the process of obtaining cheese. In the sensitivity analysis, the following composition of energy sources were consid- ered for S1 95% grid mix and 5% solar energy, 90% grid mix and 10% solar energy, 80% grid mix and 20% solar energy; for 95% grid mix and 5% wind energy, 90% grid mix and 10% wind energy, 80% grid mix and 20% wind energy; and for S3 95% grid mix and 5% biogas energy, 90% grid mix and 10% biogas energy, 80% grid mix and 20% biogas energy.
